EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front remains situated just to the northwest this morning with a light onshore flow near the surface while persistent southwesterly flow continues aloft. Cooler than normal mid-level temperatures created deeper instability that strengthened diurnally driven sea breeze storms as they develop along the east-coast sea breeze in the afternoon. Deeper moisture was across NE FL and portions of SE GA (generally south and east of Waycross) created more widespread storm coverage, especially along and west of the St Johns River basin as the sea breeze interacted with the river breeze. ||Strong to marginally severe wind gusts of 40-60 mph occurred. Marginal deep layer shear was around 20 kts which create a lower end hail threat. Locally heavy rainfall occurred that created mainly nuisance minor flood issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wake trough in trailing the departure of Hurricane Idalia lingering across NE FL. This feature combined with high tropical moisture, diurnal instability and the inland progressing west coast sea breeze produced localized heavy, flooding rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was first reported around 2:46 pm near Main Street and near the 500 block of 32rd Avenue in Gainesville. A mesonet station about 2 miles SE of Gainesville measured 2.8 inches of rainfall in one hour at 2:45 pm. At 3:14 pm, Fire Rescue reported flash flooding was developing along NW 23rd Avenue in Gainesville. At 3:16 pm, at public weather station measured around 4.5 inches of rainfall about 1 mile SE of Gainesville since 1:45 pm. At 3:35 pm, a CoCoRaHS station about 2 miles SE of Gainesville measured 4.47 inches since 1:52 pm which created flooding conditions. By 4 pm, the county 911 dispatch center reported several roads within about a 10 block radius in Gainesville experienced significant flash flooding. There were some road closures at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved through the area with ample instability and shear for severe weather. Numerous reports of severe weather were received across the tri-state area.  Two tornadoes occurred across southwest Georgia, and seven tornadoes occurred across the eastern Florida panhandle and big bend.  An EF2 tornado produced significant damage in the town of Hosford, FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado likely first touched down against the tree line along the bay across from A.L. Kinsaul Park. The initial damage included some roof damage to a gazebo where the panels were blown off. It looks like at the strongest damage point it was estimated that the tornado was 75 yards in width. Just across from the park to the northeast, the roof of a house was nearly completely destroyed. There was a carport attached to the roof which was demolished, and the roof was then lifted off and carried across maybe 20 yards across the street and impacted another house. This second house had the cars in the driveway lifted and turned a few feet. The home suffered structural damage to the front wall with cracks spreading towards the back of the home. These two homes appeared to suffered the most direct damage. Turning north down the road, the neighborhood had similar damage to siding of the homes as most of the damage came from a southwest to northeast direction. Snapped trees, patio furniture tossed, and broken windows were the most common impacts. Near the end of the path, a large shed's roof was lifted off and carried a few feet away. About 3/4 mile northeast of the start point, the tornado likely weakened as the damage becomes more minor consisting of roof siding damage and snapped twigs. On the northern sides of homes, hail damage was evident due to hail being driven onto the sides of homes on the north side of the tornado. This tornado was rated EF1 with max winds estimated at 110 mph. Total damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved through the area with ample instability and shear for severe weather. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second corridor of enhanced straight line winds was noted across the Killearn Lakes and Golden Eagle Communities. This damage area was distinct from the damage area along Lake Iamonia in that debris fell to the east-southeast. This is still indicative of straight line winds. Initial damage was noted along Bull Headley Road and homes on the eastern edge of the Luna Pines neighborhood. Several trees were uprooted or snapped in this area. The wind damage footprint became locally intense into Killearn Lakes along Log Ridge Trail and Copperfield Circle. Several large pines were snapped, with a few homes being impacted by these falling pines. Damage continued across Deerlake West at the intersection of Copperfield Circle. Numerous trees in the median were snapped, all pointing to the east-southeast. The damage area became less concentrated while moving across Lake Monkey Business, Lake Dianne, and into the Golden Eagle Community. Numerous trees were downed across the Golden Eagle Golf Course and at several residences within the community. A locally intense area of damage was noted on Deerlake E just east of the entrance gate to Golden Eagle. Several trees in this area were snapped, with one falling across Deerlake East. Based on the survey, no damage associated with this corridor of stronger winds reached Thomasville Road. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through South Florida early on the morning of January 14th, followed by much colder air moving into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell to as low as 33F at the Belle Glade FAWN location, with unofficial readings near ground level as low as 28-30F both early Sunday morning January 15th, and early Monday morning January 16th. Crop damage was mainly confined to the Everglades Agricultural Area of western Palm Beach County, and varied widely from one location to another. Leafy vegetables including lettuce had ice and frost causing peeling and burning. The most sensitive crops to these types of injury were iceberg and romaine lettuce. Some crops close to harvest might be rejected or receive complaints because of this injury and growers will have to deal with these issues. Baby leafy vegetables / spring mix nearing harvest were protected by row covers, therefore no damage was reported for these crops. Corn and bean suffered the most in this area, including Pahokee. The damage was site and crop stage specific as well. Some growers reported complete loss, some report only minor losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward across SE GA during the morning and placed the entire forecast area within the warm sector ahead of an approaching pre-frontal squall line. Strong SSW speed shear was over the area with 0-6 km bulk shear 50-60 kts across SE GA to 40-50 kts southward across NE FL. Warm and unstable conditions pushed northward to near Waycross into the afternoon with ML CAPE 1000-1500 J/kg as the pre-frontal convection approached into the late afternoon and early evening. Strong to severe storms moved across the local area during the late afternoon and early evening producing wind damage and a couple of tornadoes in SE GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherFlow mesonet sensor at Huguenot Park measured a wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong upper level shortwaves impacted the area during the latter half of January, bringing with them a couple of rounds of severe weather with numerous reports of trees and power lines blown down. In addition, a few tornadoes occurred across the tri-state area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just east of Ashley Hall Road south of Bob Miller Road at approximately 1245 pm ET. The tornado caused considerable damage to trees in the area, snapping and uprooting numerous pine and oak trees. The tornado moved northeast across Bob Miller Road and S & S Ranch Road before dissipating less than two minutes after touching down. Falling trees caused damage to several manufactured homes and automobiles along Bob Miller road. Minor roof damage was also noted at one single-family home at the corner of Dana Drive. The damage to trees was consistent with an EF1 tornado with peak winds of approximately 95 mph. No injuries were reported. Thanks to Wakulla County Emergency Management for cooperation and assistance with the damage survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed south of Lake City during the afternoon of January 19th.  A warm and breezy day developed as high pressure shifted SE of the region with the approach of a weakening front. Temperatures warmed to near 80 inland through mid afternoon despite increasing cloudiness. The main weather impact for most locations was gusty SSW winds with sustained speeds 12-16 mph and gusts of 20-30 mph into the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2:40 pm on 1/19/23, the Florida Forest Service reported a wildfire about 5 miles south of Lake City. The fire was about 12 acres around 4:18 pm local time. About 30 residences were evacuated. Gusty SW winds exacerbated fire growth. By 11:10 pm local time, the Florida Forest Service and local broadcast media reported that the fire was contained after consuming 44 acres. The cost of damage was unknown.

The tornado tracked northeast through the forest, snapping and uprooting numerous trees along its path. The tornado dissipated in the forest before reaching populated areas around Lake Munson and Capital Circle. The tornado was rated EF1 with max winds estimated at 105 mph due to the tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong upper level shortwaves impacted the area during the latter half of January, bringing with them a couple of rounds of severe weather with numerous reports of trees and power lines blown down. In addition, a few tornadoes occurred across the tri-state area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the Driftwood Estates subdivision of South Walton County between Riverbirch Loop and Cocobolo Drive. The tornado caused mainly roof damage as it crossed the subdivision. Homes suffered damage to roof coverings on Cocobolo Drive, and both ends of Pin Oak Loop. The tornado then apparently lifted briefly as it crossed a wooded area. A drone provided by South Walton Fire District could find no damage in the woods. The tornado then touched down on Red Bay Court where it snapped or damaged several small hardwood trees and did some minor roof damage. More substantial roof damage occurred to three homes on Loblolly Bay Drive and four homes on Driftwood Point Road. One sturdy palm tree was snapped at the intersection of Red Bay and Loblolly with four others partially uprooted on Driftwood Point Road. The tornado then lifted over Choctawhatchee Bay. Most of the roof damage in this community was consistent with EF-1 winds. However, three homes experienced the uplift and failure of large sections of the roof structures themselves. That is consistent with EF-2 damage with maximum winds of 115 mph.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong upper level shortwaves impacted the area during the latter half of January, bringing with them a couple of rounds of severe weather with numerous reports of trees and power lines blown down. In addition, a few tornadoes occurred across the tri-state area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at 3922 Lovewood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with a MCS over the northern peninsula produce heavy rain and flooding near Williston in Levy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WeatherSTEM site 0566W Williston reported 7.54 inches of rain over approximately 8 hour period from 2 am to 10 am EST.

An EF2 tornado produced significant damage in the town of Hosford, FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the influence of a mid-level disturbance to the north, a surface frontal boundary sagged into South-Central Florida which put South Florida near a source of lift, combined with surface instability from diurnal heating. Additional shortwave impulses added to the convective activity and led to severe thunderstorm producing a tornado in Broward County on April 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado affected southern portions of Boynton Beach on the evening of April 28th. The first visible signs of tornadic damage were just west of I-95 and south of SW 23rd Ave in the form of several broken large tree branches and a royal palm tree partially stripped of its fronds. The tornado moved ENE continuing it's path as it crossed I-95, with reports of traffic stopping or slowing down as the circulation crossed the interstate. The tornado then moved through portions of the High Point community where about a half-dozen homes sustained minor roof uplift near the corners of the structures, awnings twisted or completely torn off and landing several houses away, and several large tree branches and trunks broken. The  final indication of tornadic damage was a downed small palm tree at Main Blvd, east of SW 3rd St, just south of the Boynton Beach Little League Park. The damage path was sporadic in spots, with large tree branches or trunks snapped right next to other trees and buildings with no visible signs of damage. Therefore, the tornado is rated as an EF-0 with estimated maximum winds near 80 mph. Most of the winds along the damage path were estimated in the 65-75 mph range. Damage amounts are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following ample sunshine, mesoscale boundary collisions created afternoon and evening showers and strong thunderstorms across South Florida April 29th, especially around Lake Okeechobee and Palm Beach County. A cell merger that began in Palm Beach Gardens was able to take advantage of a favorable environment for tornadoes and spawned an EF-2 tornado in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in eastern Palm Beach Gardens, just east of Interstate 95, and moved northeast across A1A, passing just south of the Palm Beach Gardens Medical Center. As it moved through the Sanctuary Cove community, it crossed State Road 786 (PGA Boulevard) near the intersection with US-1. After a short trek up US-1, the tornado dissipated before reaching Juno Beach. Light tree damage consistent with an EF-0 was noted near the determined starting point of the tornado. The tornado strengthened quickly to an EF1 as it moved through the Gardens East apartment complex. Significant tree damage ranging from uprooted trees to snapped trunks were noted in this community. The tornado then moved through the Sandalwood Estates |community off of Burns road. Quite a bit of tree debris was scattered throughout this community, with several large trees completely uprooted. One notable damage indicator was a greater than 3 inch diameter branch that pierced the metal roof of a |residence. A countless number of large branches were stripped from trees throughout the community with a few snapped trunks near the worst of the damage in this area. The tornado then moved through the Rainwood community where a home suffered broken windows and had many shingles removed. Similar tree damage continued into this neighborhood.||When the tornado crossed Prosperity Farms Road and entered the Sanctuary Cove community, this was where it strengthened to EF-2 intensity reaching an estimated peak wind of 130 mph. Some of the most substantial structural damage was noted here with collapsed roofs, broken windows, removed roofing material, and debarking of trees. The EF2 tornado then cross the North Palm Beach Waterway where it completely destroyed a manufactured home along the canal bank. As the tornado moved NNE it crossed into the Point at Palm Beach Gardens community where it created minor structural damage to the complex, but had some of its most photographed moments as |cars were displaced, flipped, and stacked on each other. The tornado then moved through the City Centre where considerable tree, light post, and street sign damage was noted. As it crossed PGA Blvd the tornado destroyed a dry cleaners business, collapsed large light poles, and even caused a concrete electrical pole to lean. The last notable damage was still at EF2 strength when the tornado crossed US 1 and caused two cars to be flipped and displaced on the opposite side of US 1. Several videos of the |incident were witnessed, as well as one from inside the vehicle, and the survey crew was able to interview one of the drivers. The tornado quickly lifted after crossing US 1 where it caused minor tree damage in a shopping center. Damage value is estimated based on the number of structures, vehicles, and trees damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents resulted in the deaths of two individuals in Brevard County, including a 48-year-old male in Melbourne Beach, and a 24-year-old male in Indialantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indialantic Police Department relayed a report of a 24-year-old male from Osceola County who drowned after being caught in a rip current near Wave Crest Ave. in Indialantic. At 1610EST, fire rescue responded to a report of an unresponsive male in the ocean approximately 100-200 yards east of Wave Crest Ave, where he was rescued and resuscitated, but ultimately succumbed to his injuries while being transported to an area hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents resulted in the deaths of two individuals in Brevard County, including a 48-year-old male in Melbourne Beach, and a 24-year-old male in Indialantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: News media reported the drowning of a 48-year-old male from New York after his body was pulled from the surf at Ocean Ave. in Melbourne Beach. Reports indicate the man was attempting to help his son who was also caught in a rip current at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple swimmers were in distress near the Regional Beach Access of Miramar Beach in Walton county, approximately 150 yards offshore, shortly before 2 PM CST. In particular, a 20-year old male became submerged underwater. Lifeguards located the victim and brought him back to shore where he was pronounced deceased. Single red flags were flying at the time the incident and a high risk of rip currents statement was in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level trough brought rain and thunderstorms to the region. Although most storms remained below severe limits, one storm produced a brief EF1 tornado in Walton County, FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down just west of County Highway 280 East south of Pryor Road in Walton County. The tornado did significant damage to trees, uprooting and snapping numerous pines and hardwoods. Minor damage was noted to a garage and several outbuildings. The damage was consistent with an EF1 tornado with maximum winds of approximately 105 mph. We would like to thank Walton County Emergency Management for their assistance with this damage survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist easterly winds generated showers and thunderstorms over the near-shore Atlantic waters of SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various members of the broadcast media sent in video footage of a waterspout briefly |moving onshore just south of Boca Inlet at Ocean Towers. Several umbrellas and chairs |were visibly picked up in the air by the circulation before dissipating shortly afterwards.

The video footage showed a circulation briefly moving onshore with chairs and an umbrella being picked up from the beach up to the Broadwalk. A tornado warning was issued at 12:44 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and frontal boundary migrated across south Florida, which including daytime heating, brought multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms to the east coast metro in South Florida April 16th and overnight into the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph/52 knots was measured at the Belle Glade FAWN mesonet station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening frontal boundary approached and stalled on the Florida Gulf coast in the morning of April 14. The increasing instability, with warm temperatures, in the afternoon led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeastern Florida in the late afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Earth Networks mesonet site at Belen Jesuit Preparatory School measured a wind gust of 61 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through the Tamiami area. A report on social media showed a large tree uprooted about 1 mile to the SE near Wesley Matthews Elementary School during the same thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 24th, a minor shortwave trough funneled plentiful moisture into the region. This allowed for numerous scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to bring localized flooding from the rainfall and locally gusty winds, especially in the eastern metro of South Florida throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a measured wind gust of 56 mph in Weston. A member of the media shared an image of several large tree branches downed by winds in the same area. Hail up to a half-inch in diameter was also reported with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Hurricane Idalia passed about 165 nautical miles to the west of Key West on a northward path through the eastern Gulf of Mexico.  Tropical storm-force wind gusts occurred in squalls which passed mainly through portions of the Lower Florida Keys.  The maximum winds and rainfall occurred with the passage of one strong rainband which resulted in isolated fallen trees and tree limbs, isolated power outages primarily affecting a street block or two at a time. The combination of an approaching spring tide and strong southerly winds sustained just below tropical storm force resulted in storm tides ranging from 1.0 to 2.5 feet above MHHW along the oceanside.  Moderate to heavy ocean wave overwash and street flooding within a few oceanside neighborhoods on the southwest portion of Key West occurred within a few hours of high tide on the mornings of August 29th and 30th, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The majority of wind impacts occurred with a single rain band that passed through the Middle Florida Keys during the afternoon hours of August 29, 2023.  Tropical storm-force wind gusts produced isolated power outages due to tree limbs contacting power lines mainly affect fewer than 20 customers at a time.  Peak sustained winds were measured at 33 knots at Duck Key with maximum gusts to 39 knots at the Florida Keys Marathon International Airport.

The maximum sustained winds were measured at 22 knots with gusts to 38 knots at an electrical substation in Tavernier, FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the Florida panhandle and produced large hail.
Prolonged periods of dry weather across much of South Florida during the winter months led to the development of severe to extreme drought conditions during the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a record dry February with no measurable rainfall in Naples and Marco Island, March remained dry with only 0.30 inches of rain measured at Naples Municipal Airport and 0.84 in Marco Island. These prolonged dry conditions led to the designation of severe drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor, and eventually worsened to extreme drought (D3) on March 28th. A burn ban was implemented for Collier County effective March 4th.

These prolonged dry conditions led to the designation of severe drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor, and eventually worsened to extreme drought (D3) on March 28th. A burn ban was implemented for Hendry County effective March 8th.

Tropical storm-force wind gusts occurred in squalls which passed mainly through portions of the Lower Florida Keys.  The maximum winds and rainfall occurred with the passage of one strong rainband which resulted in isolated fallen trees and tree limbs, isolated power outages primarily affecting a street block or two at a time. The combination of an approaching spring tide and strong southerly winds sustained just below tropical storm force resulted in storm tides ranging from 1.0 to 2.5 feet above MHHW along the oceanside.  Moderate to heavy ocean wave overwash and street flooding within a few oceanside neighborhoods on the southwest portion of Key West occurred within a few hours of high tide on the mornings of August 29th and 30th, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The center of Hurricane Idalia passed about 165 nautical miles to the west of Key West on a northward path through the eastern Gulf of Mexico.  The combination of an approaching spring tide and strong southerly winds sustained just below tropical storm force resulted in storm tides ranging from 1.0 to 2.5 feet above MHHW along the oceanside of the extreme western Lower Florida Keys.  Moderate to heavy ocean wave overwash and street flooding within a few oceanside neighborhoods on the southwest portion of Key West occurred within a few hours of high tide on the mornings of August 29th and 30th, 2023.  The maximum water level was measured at 1.47 feet MHHW around 8:36 AM EST at the NOAA NOS tide gauge in Key West harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front remains situated just to the northwest this morning with a light onshore flow near the surface while persistent southwesterly flow continues aloft. Cooler than normal mid-level temperatures created deeper instability that strengthened diurnally driven sea breeze storms as they develop along the east-coast sea breeze in the afternoon. Deeper moisture was across NE FL and portions of SE GA (generally south and east of Waycross) created more widespread storm coverage, especially along and west of the St Johns River basin as the sea breeze interacted with the river breeze. ||Strong to marginally severe wind gusts of 40-60 mph occurred. Marginal deep layer shear was around 20 kts which create a lower end hail threat. Locally heavy rainfall occurred that created mainly nuisance minor flood issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large strip mall sign was blown off causing significant damage to the roof of the store at the intersection of Blanding Blvd and Shirley Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of tropical moisture and a slow moving cold front allowed for scattered showers and thunderstorms across southern Florida throughout the day. Heavier showers arrived late in the afternoon and evening, bringing rain to areas across Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A post on social media showed notable street flooding in Miami. The roadway near the intersection of Biscayne Boulevard and NE 15th Street had an estimated five inches of water across the road. Rainfall measurements in the general area were in the two to three inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal zone was meandering near the I-10 corridor in the afternoon under zonal flow aloft and passing weak short wave PVA. Cold temperatures aloft combined with shear and CAPE in the hail growth zone combined with diurnal instability and mesoscale boundary collisions to produce large hail in scattered severe afternoon and evening storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped at its base near Hilliard. A photo was shared via Social Media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to pea size hail was observed at the intersection of US 17 and Interstate 295.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Melbourne survey confirms that straight-line winds produced damage to the Groveland Mobile Home Park and Lamplighter Village retirement community along John Rodes Blvd in West Melbourne. Peak winds were estimated to be between 70 to 75 mph. A personal weather station on the east side of Lamplighter Village reported a wind gust of 71 mph at 323 pm EDT. ||Damaging winds began at the intersection of Interstate 95 and Eau Gallie Blvd. near the entrance to the Groveland Mobile Home Park, where several properties had portions of their awnings and siding torn back or removed. One home, which appeared to be under construction and unoccupied, lost a portion of its roof. ||The severe thunderstorm continued southward into the Lamplighter Village retirement|community, where approximately 50 homes were affected with at least minor damage. These homes suffered partial damage to carports, awnings, and siding. Several lake front homes along Lamplighter Dr. lost their carport and porch awnings, and one experienced major damage when a large oak tree fell onto the residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cypress Camp Trail Wildfire burned nearly 10,000 acres in Big Cypress National Preserve in early April. With high relative humidity and suppression efforts, containment was completed by mid-April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cypress Camp Trail Wildfire was first reported on April 4th approximately one mile north of Interstate 75 near Mile Marker 68 in Collier County. By April 10th, the wildfire burned 9,749 acres of Big Cypress National Preserve and was 81% contained. The official date of full containment is not known. Effective April 21, the national preserve reopened numerous closures for recreational use. These included three campgrounds, several trails, and roadways. There were no reported damages to personal properties, injuries, or fatalities.

Surf heights were 1-2 feet. A moderately strong rip current was observed about 100 yards near where the persons entered the water. The victims were not good swimmers and were not from the local area. The time of the incident was approximated based on media video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonal local Nor'Easter weather event developed April 9th into the 10th as an area of low pressure formed offshore of the central FL Atlantic coast along a lingering frontal zone as high pressure strengthened north of the region with an axis 'wedged' southward across inland GA. Strong NNE wind gusts range from 35 to 49 mph across coastal counties and the St. Johns River basin with the peak of the winds occurring during the evening of April 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong non-convective wind gusts between 40-50 mph caused minor wind damage in coastal Flagler county including many large tree limbs blown down, wind damage to shingles and fences blown down in Palm Coast.  The highest measured wind gusts was 49 mph at the Marineland mesonet station at 6:10 pm on 4/9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significantly drier than normal conditions since the winter months lingered into the first half of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lack of significant rainfall over the previous 3 months led to the continuation of severe drought (D2) conditions over inland Miami-Dade County in the beginning of April. About 2 to 3 inches of rain was measured in many areas from January 1st through March 31st. This was approximately 25 to 50% of the normal value for that time period. Increasing rainfall associated with cold fronts and upper air disturbances during the second and third weeks of April led to conditions improving to moderate drought (D1) conditions by April 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fading MCS of strong thunderstorms moved across SE GA during the late afternoon and early evening ahead. This system produced a strong outflow boundary with wind gusts of 40-45 mph that pushed southward across NE FL during the late evening which produced minor wind damage in Alachua County. An official observation of the outflow boundary was 44 mph at the Keystone Heights Airport (Larkin Field) around 1235 am on 4/16/2023 (which was about 1 hr after the wind damage occurred in Alachua County, to the NW of the airfield).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Melbourne damage survey confirms that|a brief EF-0 tornado touched down initially in the River Groves|mobile home community in Micco. The tornado continued to the east-|southeast into the Summit Cove Condominiums complex before|crossing US-1 and emerging into the Indian River Lagoon near the|Sebastian River Marina and Boatyard. The tornado was on the|ground for approximately 0.5 miles, had a maximum width of|approximately 100 yards, and peak winds estimated between 65-75|mph.||Damage was first noted near Love Ct. where the tornado touched|down at approximately 536 pm EDT. Brevard County Emergency|Management conducted an initial assessment and documented between|25-30 structures with at least minor damage in the River Groves|community, primarily along Love Ct., Cherish Dr., and River Groves|Dr. These homes suffered partial damage to carports, awnings,|siding, and skirting. Additionally, several structures lost|portions of their roof when the attached carport or porch awnings|were torn away from the home.||The tornado continued east-southeast toward the Summit Cove|Condominiums complex where some damage was noted to trees near|the clubhouse, as well as siding on nearby homes, on Summit Cove|Dr. The system crossed US-1 before emerging into the Indian River|Lagoon near the Sebastian River Marina & Boatyard, where several|boats were moved from their storage rack. An eyewitness indicates the|tornado briefly became a waterspout before dissipating.||There are no known direct injuries or fatalities as a result of|the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions over SW Florida led to continued severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3) conditions over a majority of Collier, Hendry, and Glades counties in April 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lack of significant rainfall over the previous 3 months led to the continuation of extreme drought (D3) conditions over the western half Collier County in the beginning of April. Less than 2 inches of rain was measured in many areas from January 1st through March 31st. This was approximately 10 to 25% of the normal value for that time period. Increasing rainfall around the middle of April led to conditions improving to severe drought (D2) conditions with a total of 3-5 of rain throughout the month. A burn ban remained in place for all of Collier County through the month of April.

Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport, causing mainly fence and tree damage to the surrounding neighborhood. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front began to move slowly northward across South Florida on April 12th, focusing deep moisture over the region. An mid to upper level low pressure over the NW Gulf of Mexico and an upper level jet stream over the eastern Gulf of Mexico contributed to providing the necessary atmospheric forcing for areas of persistent heavy rain and strong thunderstorms across most of SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of April 12th, prolific rainfall occurred across southern and central metro Broward County, including the cities of Fort Lauderdale, Hollywood, Dania Beach, and Wilton Manors. Showers and thunderstorms repeatedly training over this urban corridor brought multiple hours with rainfall rates of 3-6 inches/hour, with total rainfall of 15-25 inches between Noon and Midnight. The highest measured rainfall amount was 25.91 at a WeatherSTEM gauge at Fort Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport, with 20 inches falling between 4 and 10 PM. This rainfall rate has less than a 0.1% chance of being exceeded in any given year, and the rainfall over most of the Fort Lauderdale and Hollywood areas has an annual exceedance probability of no more than 0.5%. ||This extremely rare rainfall led to devastating impacts and crippled neighborhoods for several days. Fort Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport was closed for nearly 40 hours due to high standing water. Over 1,100 buildings in Fort Lauderdale suffered damage, with 766 having major damage. Fort Lauderdale City Hall sustained major water damage and was considered a complete loss. At least 38,000 cubic yards of debris was collected in Fort Lauderdale. The city of Hollywood Waste Water plant sustained damage. Damage surveys revealed high water marks of over 3 feet inside homes in the Edgewood and Melrose Park neighborhoods of Fort Lauderdale, with 1-2 feet of standing water and high water marks in sections of Dania Beach and Hollywood. About 600 residents were displaced by the flood, with many being rescued from their homes by first responders. 22,000 residents were without power during the peak of the event, with the hardest hit areas remaining without it for days. Port Everglades, which supplies 100% of the fuel for the area (13 million gallons per day), suffered a 50% reduction in distribution due to submerged fuel pumps, with interruptions lasting over 10 days. This led to a fuel shortage across the area, with most South Florida gas stations having to close for days at a time. Nearly one thousand calls were made to local towing companies for abandoned/stranded vehicles in flooded water, with the number of vehicle insurance claims across the area in the thousands.||Damage totals were provided by Fort Lauderdale and Hollywood emergency management officials.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from the April 12th flash flood event lingered for several days across parts of Fort Lauderdale, including Edgewood, Downtown, Croissant Park, Poinciana Park, and Melrose Park neighborhoods, as well as the Fort Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport. Many of these neighborhoods were stranded and inaccessible for a few days due to deep standing water on streets and inside homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of rainfall, some heavy, which began in April, continued at the beginning of May. These rains helped to improve spring drought conditions across Southwest Florida.

The Sandy Wildfire burned nearly 20,000 acres in Big Cypress National Preserve from early to mid-May. With high relative humidity and suppression efforts, containment was completed by mid-May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandy Wildfire was first reported on May 1st approximately five miles north of US-41 near Mile Marker 56 in Collier County. By May 14th, the wildfire burned 19,814 acres of Big Cypress National Preserve and was 45% contained. The official date of full containment was May 16th. The wildfire forced closures of US-41 and several recreational trails due to smoke. There were no reported damages to personal properties, injuries, or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of road closures, including a 40-mile stretch along Highway US-27, due to smoke from the Mile 31 Wildfire. The source of the fire is unknown. The total acreage of the fire was 13,500 acres.

Evacuation orders were in effect for residences between 18th Avenue NE and Jung Blvd. The fire caused the loss of two homes, an additional three homes sustaining damage, and multiple outbuildings damaged. The source of the fire was downed power lines. The total acreage of the fire was 330 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged periods of dry weather across much of South Florida during the winter months led to the development of severe drought conditions during the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a dry winter, only 0.12 inches of rain fell at Palm Beach International Airport during the month of March, and only 1.05 inches in Palm Beach Gardens. This led to the development of severe drought (D2) conditions from north to south during the final 10 days of March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two severe thunderstorms produced wind damage in Okeechobee and Indian River Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An analysis of radar imagery, photos, and videos as well as a damage assessment conducted by the Okeechobee County Emergency Management indicate that a straight-line wind event caused damage in the City of Okeechobee around 620 pm EDT on Thursday, Jun 15, 2023. Peak winds during the event were estimated between 65 to 75|mph.||Damaging winds began near the intersection of US Hwy 441 and SE 37th Ct where a fast food restaurant drive-thru awning was lifted and bent. A swath of damage continued toward the northeast across US Hwy 441, where a small section of the roof of a grocery store was torn back. Additionally, a mobile home in the nearby community of Heritage Village lost a part of its roof when its porch awning was torn away by the strong winds. Minor damage was reported at the local flea market facility where some of the metal sheathing of the roof, and a small barn, were blown into the adjacent field and across SE 28th St. Several fallen trees and a downed powerline were also confirmed by fire and emergency management officials.

Isolated strong to severe storms expanded north and east across NE FL and SE GA into the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and blocked the right lane of I-75 southbound in Reddick. A photo was shared via social media. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off upper level low and upper level trough lingered over the SE states. Moist, SW steering flow around the eastern side of this upper level feature focused embedded short wave troughs rotating through the base of the upper level trough across the local forecast area, which enhanced waves of storminess that moved inland from the Gulf of Mexico and traversed the local area through the afternoon and evening. A flood watch was in effect for portions of north-central Florida given recent heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down and there was some roof damage. The time of the damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moving offshore produced severe wind gusts near Cape Canaveral and in Lake County and a tornado in South Patrick Shores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Melbourne damage survey confirms that an EF-1 tornado impacted the South Patrick Shores community just south of Patrick Space Force Base. The tornado touched down initially in the Lighthouse Landing subdivision near the intersection of Lighthouse Landing St. and South Patrick Dr. The circulation continued on an east-northeast trajectory across the South Patrick Shores community before emerging into the Atlantic near Ocean Blvd. and SR A1A. The tornado was on the ground for approximately 1.0 mile, had a maximum width of approximately 300 yards, and peak winds estimated between 90-100 mph.||Brevard County Emergency Management conducted a damage assessment and noted numerous homes were affected with at least some damage, 25 homes with minor damage, and 10 with major damage. NWS meteorologists documented impacts on both the north and south sides of the tornadic circulation in an area bounded by SE 1st St. to the south and NE 2nd St. to the north. Numerous homes in this area experienced at least partial losses of their carports and awnings. Additionally, several homes lost portions of their roof, a few trees were uprooted, and between 6-10 power poles were snapped in this area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida FDEM Watch office relayed a report of a tree blown down onto State Road 26 which caused a partial road closure. Peak wind gusts from the nearby Gainesville ASOS were 44 mph around the time of the damage. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm south of Orlando produced quarter size hail in Osceola County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports quarter size hail in Buena Ventura Lakes near the intersection of Simpson Rd and Pebble Pointe Way. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving offshore produced a severe wind gust and a funnel cloud near Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports and pictures received via social media of a funnel cloud that developed near the intersection of the Samuel C Phillips Parkway and Spin Test Rd over the southern portion of Cape Canaveral Space Force Station. The funnel lasted two to three minutes as it moved east-southeast with no reports of the funnel touching the ground. Location and motion estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving offshore produced a severe wind gust and a funnel cloud near Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Space Force tower 0020 measured a 52 knot wind gust from the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct short-wave troughs embedded within an upper-level trough west of the region brought 2 rounds of strong to severe storms across the local area under active SW flow. The first round of storms moved inland across the Suwannee River Valley in the morning and tracked eastward across the area through early afternoon. The next round of storm occurred during the evening toward the Atlantic coast. Widespread wind gusts of 40-50 mph and small hail occurred with many of the strong storms with large hail from severe storms and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed in Espanola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was shifting south across Georgia with a surge in moist WSW flow off of the Gulf of Mexico under a passing short wave trough and bulk shear of 20-25 kts. Intrusion of drier air from the NW exacerbated DCAPE values well over 1000 J/kg and supported strong downburst winds in scattered severe storms that developed across NE FL in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell embedded within a line of thunderstorms associated with a mesoscale convective complex moving across southwest Florida produces a brief tornado on Pine Island in Lee County, while flooding reported around Cape Coral in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A virtual survey using several photos captured from the area suggests that the only damage was done to a recently-constructed metal outdoor freezer. This freezer was complete, properly secured, and was made of aluminum and insulated with Styrofoam. |While some fence damage was noted, this damage could not be reliably attributed to the tornado, given a lack of nearby tree damage and little-to-debris. Imagery from the area also shows some damage to this fence before the tornado took place. As such, the |tornado was on the ground for approximately 27 feet (or 0.005 mi) and spent only a few brief seconds on the ground before lifting again.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long period easterly ocean swell that originated from former Tropical Storm Cindy  combined with breezy onshore winds to keep a higher end moderate risk in place at northeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fernandina Beach lifeguards reported strong rip currents in the surf zone. Three rescues occurred before noon. NWS JAX upgraded to a rip current risk later in the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low NW of the region began to lift northward through the day, while a brisk SW steering continued to stream high moisture from the Gulf of Mexico across NE FL. The combination of the flow, moisture, diurnal instability and a passing embedded short wave trough aloft produced isolated strong storms. A flood watch was in effect for portions of NE FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gilchrist County Fire Rescue reported storm damage along NW 72nd PL near Bell. A significant portion of a metal roof was peeled off an outdoor shelter. Two large trees also snapped at the trunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another high rip current risk day for NE Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fernandina Beach lifeguards had 10 rip current rescues.

Isolated strong to severe storms also formed in the afternoon with the approach of another short-wave mid-level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a funnel cloud descending from a rotating wall cloud with a supercell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist S to SW wind flow combined with low pressure aloft over the SE United States led to the development of afternoon showers and thunderstorms across SE Florida, some of which were severe over Miami-Dade County.

Other reports indicated southbound lanes of the Palmetto Expressway flooded, as well as the exit ramp from State Road 836 (Dolphin Expressway) to the Palmetto Expressway. Rainfall estimates of 4 to 5 inches in the area during the time of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the SE United States and a SE wind flow across South Florida led to the development of strong thunderstorms over the interior portions of SW Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee reported hail up to 1.25 in diameter in Ave Maria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow for a couple of days continued a high-end moderate rip current risk at local area beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 19 year old male visiting the beach attempted to rescue 2 persons around 8 pm from the water. The male began to struggle in the surf due to rip currents. There were no lifeguards on duty. Surf was reported around 2-4 ft. A moderate rip current risk was headlined in NWS Jacksonville products.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dominant west coast sea breeze regime triggered scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon, with strong to pulse severe storms developing toward the Atlantic coast where boundaries merged. Steep low level lapse rates and high CAPE supported hail and strong wind gusts in stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed just NE of Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled southward across SE GA and over NE FL into the afternoon. This boundary interacted with the east coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass to produce isolated strong to pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed along Kevin Drive in Orange Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled southward across SE GA and over NE FL into the afternoon. This boundary interacted with the east coast sea breeze over a moist and unstable airmass to produce isolated strong to pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public network mesonet station measured 3.17 inches of daily rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along South County Road 315. The time of the event was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prevailing SW steering flow brough a more dominant west coast sea breeze inland where it converged with the east coast sea breeze toward the Atlantic coast in the evening and produced strong to pulse severe storms over a moist and unstable airmass. DCAPE were high in the 700-1000 J/kg range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Naval Station Mayport measured a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front lingered across South Georgia. This feature combined with elevated MLCAPE of 2000 J/kg, steep mid-level lapse rates, decent shear and moist dew pts produced scattered strong to severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to dime size hail was reported in Maxville. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough west of the region focused a series of short-wave troughs across the local area. The upper-level forcing combined with high moisture combined with a low level 850 mb jet of 35 kts enhanced morning convection across NE FL. Isolated strong to severe storms expanded north and east across NE FL and SE GA into the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station near Citra measured 2.15 inches of rainfall since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow waterspout developed off the south shore of Key West, and moved north, making landfall as a tornado at Higgs Beach of Key West.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow waterspout with a spray ring extending one-quarter the way up from the water surface to cloud base moved north onshore Higgs Beach as a tornado.  The tornado dissipated on the beach without any discernable damage noted.  Maximum wind speeds of 50 to 60 mph were estimated at the landfall location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak high pressure set up over the western Atlantic Ocean, there was a shift to a low level east-southeasterly flow. This resulted in scattered morning showers and storms near the southeastern Florida coast. Gusty winds and locally heavy rains were the two main hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video posted on social media showed street flooding at the intersection of SW 7th St             and SW 2nd Ave in Miami due to heavy downpours from a strong thunderstorm. The             roadway is covered in an estimated 6 inches of water up to curb level. Nearby rainfall observations indicated between 2 and 3 inches of rain in a 2-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal system positioned over the southeastern United States led to plentiful moisture and instability which resulted in conditions for numerous showers and thunderstorms throughout the day across southern Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported a funnel cloud with a passing severe thunderstorm over the Doral area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico resulted in a slow-moving pre-frontal trough focusing heavy convective rainfall over portions of the middle and upper Florida Keys.  One EF0 tornado was observed on Big Pine Key in association with a small supercell which evolved in advance of the passing squall line. In additional, strong winds associated with a convectively-induced mesoscale low pressure system over the middle and upper Florida Keys resulted in moderate to major coastal flooding near the shores of Barnes and Card Sounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall estimated by radar of 5 to 8 inches produced extensive poor drainage area flooding across Marathon and Key Colony Beach, with some streets with one to two feet depth of water.  Several motor vehicles stalled due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico resulted in a slow-moving pre-frontal trough focusing heavy convective rainfall over portions of the middle and upper Florida Keys.  One EF0 tornado was observed on Big Pine Key in association with a small supercell which evolved in advance of the passing squall line. In additional, strong winds associated with a convectively-induced mesoscale low pressure system over the middle and upper Florida Keys resulted in moderate to major coastal flooding near the shores of Barnes and Card Sounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A northeast wind gust of 57 knots was measured at the Ocean Reef Club Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary frontal system positioned over the southeastern United States led to plentiful moisture and instability which resulted in conditions for numerous showers and thunderstorms throughout the day across southern Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed minor street flooding near the intersection of Biscayne             Blvd. and NE 13th Street in Downtown Miami. Estimated 6 to 8 inch maximum water level. Rainfall around 2 inches in less than an hour was estimated in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a frontal boundary sits to the north in the Florida Peninsula, there was abundant moisture across southern Florida. With diurnally driven instability and plentiful moisture, there were numerous heavy downpours and thunderstorms. The slow-moving storms across the east coast led to several inches of rain in the eastern metropolitan corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami Beach Public Works reported water entering a parking garage in Mid-Beach. There were other reports of street flooding in South Beach. An estimated two to three inches of rain fell across Miami Beach with the evening thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was across the southeast region with an upper-level trough across the deep south. Passing short wave troughs combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered several waves of strong to severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on a home causing roof damage on the Jacksonville Northside along Briarcliff Road. Powerlines were also reported down in the northside area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was across the southeast region with an upper-level trough across the deep south. Passing short wave troughs combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered several waves of strong to severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down in the Arlington area. A large tree fell and broke a fence and another large tree fell on parked cars near Lake Lucina Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was across the southeast region with an upper-level trough across the deep south. Passing short wave troughs combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered several waves of strong to severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across Hilliard. A large tree fell onto a house and a truck along Conner Road. Tree caused significant structural damage to the roof and walls. The truck sustained significant damage to the bed of the truck and the gas tank cap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level impulse passed NW to SE across the local area during the morning hours with phased low-level instability nosing inland from the Gulf of Mexico producing scattered strong to severe storms across NE FL.  Isolated strong to severe storms also formed in the afternoon with the approach of another short-wave mid-level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree uprooted and fell onto a car at an apartment complex near 62nd Blvd and SW 4th Plaza. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two distinct short-wave troughs embedded within an upper-level trough west of the region brought 2 rounds of strong to severe storms across the local area under active SW flow. The first round of storms moved inland across the Suwannee River Valley in the morning and tracked eastward across the area through early afternoon. The next round of storm occurred during the evening toward the Atlantic coast. Widespread wind gusts of 40-50 mph and small hail occurred with many of the strong storms with large hail from severe storms and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree snapped at the base and fell onto a house at the intersection of Colbert Lane at North Village Parkway. Unknown damage to the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was shifting south across Georgia with a surge in moist WSW flow off of the Gulf of Mexico under a passing short wave trough and bulk shear of 20-25 kts. Intrusion of drier air from the NW exacerbated DCAPE values well over 1000 J/kg and supported strong downburst winds in scattered severe storms that developed across NE FL in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down near glen St. Mary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was shifting south across Georgia with a surge in moist WSW flow off of the Gulf of Mexico under a passing short wave trough and bulk shear of 20-25 kts. Intrusion of drier air from the NW exacerbated DCAPE values well over 1000 J/kg and supported strong downburst winds in scattered severe storms that developed across NE FL in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along Country Estates Ct.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto powerlines near the intersection of Holly Ridge Dr and Moody Ave. The downed tree also caused a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was shifting south across Georgia with a surge in moist WSW flow off of the Gulf of Mexico under a passing short wave trough and bulk shear of 20-25 kts. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and snapped at the base along State Road 13 which caused power outages around San Juan Del Rio Catholic Church. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough west of the region focused a series of short-wave troughs across the local area. The upper-level forcing combined with high moisture combined with a low level 850 mb jet of 35 kts enhanced morning convection across NE FL. Isolated strong to severe storms expanded north and east across NE FL and SE GA into the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Reddick measured storm total rainfall of 2.19 inches since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the backside of an exiting frontal boundary, there was a round of showers and thunderstorms before the dry air entered the region. The primary area where the activity occurred was on the southeastern portion of Florida, especially Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a large tree down after strong thunderstorm passes with gusty winds. Numerous reports of winds reaching 45 to 60 mph across areas impacted by the same thunderstorms in Broward County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough west of the region focused a series of short-wave troughs across the local area. The upper-level forcing combined with high moisture combined with a low level 850 mb jet of 35 kts enhanced morning convection across NE FL. Isolated strong to severe storms expanded north and east across NE FL and SE GA into the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred within the Ocala city limits from about 5-6 inches of rainfall during the morning hours during about a 3 hour accumulation period. The first flash flood report to the NWS was at 9:42 am when Marion county dispatch reported that 3 lanes of traffic were closed on Southeast 17th Street near Southeast Lake Weir Avenue. At 10:15 am, a local newspaper reported that East Silver Springs Blvd, west of South Pine Avenue, was flooding with some portions of the road closed due to flood water. At 10:34 am, the public reported a car was stuck in flood water under bridge on U.S. Highway 441 south of Southeast 3rd Avenue. Around 3 to 4 inches of rainfall was measured in this area around the time of this report since about 8 am. Police and emergency vehicles were on scene. On 6/21 around 9:32 am, law enforcement reported that County Road 475A (SW 27th Avenue) in Ocala was closed due to flood water from SW 66th Street to SW 42nd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the backside of an exiting frontal boundary, there was a round of showers and thunderstorms before the dry air entered the region. The primary area where the activity occurred was on the southeastern portion of Florida, especially Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets impassable in Weston from very heavy rain with some cars stalled. Rainfall of 4-5 inches fell in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep flow of tropical moisture continued over South Florida associated with Tropical Storm Arlene over the NE Gulf of Mexico. This along with instability aided by a jet stream aloft led to the formation of strong and severe thunderstorms across SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 83 mph at the WeatherFlow station 1694W at Ft Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport.||Some empty cargo containers were blown around by the wind and hit the airport perimeter fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep flow of tropical moisture continued over South Florida associated with Tropical Storm Arlene over the NE Gulf of Mexico. This along with instability aided by a jet stream aloft led to the formation of strong and severe thunderstorms across SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: City of Hollywood emergency management reported quarter-sized hail and trees down in the Hollywood Hills area. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep flow of tropical moisture continued over South Florida associated with Tropical Storm Arlene over the NE Gulf of Mexico. This along with instability aided by a jet stream aloft led to the formation of strong and severe thunderstorms across SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported at Marina Mile Boulevard and SW 9th Avenue. At nearby Fort Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport, a couple of cars had hail damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was shifting south across Georgia with a surge in moist WSW flow off of the Gulf of Mexico under a passing short wave trough and bulk shear of 20-25 kts. Intrusion of drier air from the NW exacerbated DCAPE values well over 1000 J/kg and supported strong downburst winds in scattered severe storms that developed across NE FL in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A medium size tree was blown down. A fence and pole barn were damaged by strong winds on the 700 block of Arthur Moore Drive. A large tree was down on power lines at Arthur Moore Drive and Branscomb Drive. A branch was blown into power lines in the 1000 block of Arthur Moore Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One man drowned and one man was injured in a rip current at Daytona Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two brothers from Pierson were rescued from the ocean just north of the Daytona Beach Pier and were transported to the hospital in critical condition. The 22 year-old man succumbed to his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rip current led to one fatality in north Daytona Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three visitors were caught in a rip current in an unguarded portion of the beach in the 1600 block of north Daytona Beach. Two made it to shore, but the third, a man in his 40s, was recused unresponsive and could not be revived. All three were transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the influence of a mid-level trough to the north, South Florida was provided with a source of lift, which combined with surface instability from diurnal heating to develop strong to severe thunderstorms on the afternoon of July 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a house fire in North Lauderdale along SW 81 Ave and 4th Court as a thunderstorm was affecting the area. Lightning data showed at least 2 strikes in very close proximity to the location at around 420 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the backside of an exiting frontal boundary, there was a round of showers and thunderstorms before the dry air entered the region. The primary area where the activity occurred was on the southeastern portion of Florida, especially Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph/50 knots was measured at WeatherSTEM station 1529W City Of Sunrise Fire as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure over the eastern Gulf of Mexico resulted in a slow-moving pre-frontal trough focusing heavy convective rainfall over portions of the middle and upper Florida Keys.  One EF0 tornado was observed on Big Pine Key in association with a small supercell which evolved in advance of the passing squall line. In additional, strong winds associated with a convectively-induced mesoscale low pressure system over the middle and upper Florida Keys resulted in moderate to major coastal flooding near the shores of Barnes and Card Sounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida DOT and Monroe County Law Enforcement reported flooding of US-1 near Mile Marker 91 in Tavernier.  Northbound lanes were nearly impassible for about one hour.  Later rainfall estimates from CoCoRAHS observers reported approximately 7 to 10 inches of rainfall by the following morning.

In additional, strong winds associated with a convectively-induced mesoscale low pressure system over the middle and upper Florida Keys resulted in moderate to major coastal flooding near the shores of Barnes and Card Sounds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal Flooding observed by Monroe County law enforcement, fire rescue, and Florida DOT over Card Sound Road west of the 3-way stop to just east of Card Sound Bridge, as well as for about a half mile west of Card Sound Bridge, reaching 6 to 12 inches of depth and requiring temporary closure.  Coastal flooding was also observed 1 to 2 feet deep in the Manatee Bay subdivision, and was about 6 to 12 inches deep over an exposed area of northbound U.S. 1 near Barnes Sound due to wave action.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist S to SW wind flow combined with low pressure aloft over the SE United States led to the development of afternoon showers and thunderstorms across SE Florida, some of which were severe over Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines downed and large tree limbs snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado initially developed over the |southeast edge of the Eden Pines subdivision, located over the|central portion of (the island of) Big Pine Key around 6:27 AM EST.|The relatively narrow tornado moved quickly north-northwest in |the general area framed by Lantana Lane, Buttonwood and Oleander|Drives. EF-0 damage was observed via the survey included vinyl|siding torn off a portion of a home, a couple downed and snapped|buttonwood trees, and a twisted fence.  Maximum winds of 75 mph were estimated in this portion of the Eden Pines subdivision.  The directional deposition|despite relatively low volume of debris strongly suggested a |tornadic circulation at the surface along a short and narrow path.  |A resident heard a distinct audible rushing sound almost |immediately before tornado passage, notable due to the lack of any|observed wind damage off the path within the residential |subdivision. The timing of the original observation of wind |damage corresponded with the passage of a supercell on a northwest |path across central Big Pine Key and into Pine Channel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist, unsettled SW steering flow and low-level convergence brought an early start to storms across the Suwannee River Valley. A short-wave trough impulse approached from the west in the afternoon over an unstable airmass, that further enhanced convective growth as storms approached the Atlantic coast. A strengthening low-level jet prevented the east coast sea breeze from moving far inland but did enhance downdraft strength in storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave troughs crossing the region, and cooler temperatures aloft, led to the ideal environment for an active afternoon and evening on July 13th with widespread showers and thunderstorms. Ample diurnal heating increased instability across the region which led to numerous thunderstorms with the primary hazard of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS Station KPBI at West Palm Beach International Airport measured a wind gust of 58 knots / 67 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall just to the north of the local area near Keaton Beach, FL in Taylor county in Florida's Big Bend as a category 3 hurricane and brought devastating storm surge and wind impacts all along the west Florida coast, especially in closer vicinity to the landfall area across the Nature Coast. Storm surge flooding reached into homes and businesses as far south as Lee county, and the surge inundation is the highest on record for the Cedar Key area.||County-by-County Impacts||Charlotte County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 55 to 65 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph near Charlotte Harbor at 1:49 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.11 inches near Harbour Heights. A peak water level of 3.57 feet above MHHW was measured at North Port Charlotte at 5:45 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Charlotte County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. US 41 was closed at Peace River Bridge due to flooding in downtown Punta Gorda, and 12 roadways were impassable due to saltwater inundation. 66 structures had major damage, and 14 had minor damage, with power outages affecting over 14,000.||Citrus County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 64 mph near Crystal River at 5:15 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 5 inches, with a maximum total of 5.2 inches near Inverness. A peak water level of 7.01 feet above MHHW was measured at the mouth of Crystal River near Shell Island at 8:45 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Citrus County generally ranged from 4 to 7 feet. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated numerous roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. Over 6,450 structures had inundation, with 72% being residential, 20% multi-family, and 8% commercial.||DeSoto County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 30 to 40 mph, with a maximum gust of 35 mph near Arcadia at 12:53 PM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 5 to 8 inches, with a maximum total of 7.11 inches near Arcadia. Power outages impacted 3,390.||Hardee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 4.64 inches near Ona.||Hernando County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 50 to 60 mph, with a maximum gust of 58 mph at Brooksville-Tampa Bay Regional Airport at 6:03 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.88 inches near Spring Hill. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moving offshore produced a severe wind gust at Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USSF Tower 0714 measured a 56 knot wind gust from the west-southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high rip current risk was in effect for local beaches given onshore flow, elevated swell periods and surf in the 2-3 ft range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Johns County Ocean Rescue received a call at 7 pm (guards are off duty at 6 pm) of two people drowning just north of Mickler's Landing in St. Johns County. One was a 42-year-old male and the other a juvenile. The adult was a fatality. The child was believed to be taken to the hospital. Responders reported rip currents and surf heights of around 2 feet. According to the bystanders, the child went into distress first and then the adult attempted to assist.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high rip current risk was in effect for local beaches given onshore flow, elevated swell periods and surf in the 2-3 ft range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fernandina Beach lifeguards reported 6 rip current rescues. Surf was 2-3 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper-level trough moved over the Florida Peninsula, abundant low-level moisture and diurnal heating led to plenty of instability in the atmosphere on July 16th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms formed in the afternoon and evening across South Florida with the primary hazard of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station in Sunrise near NW 96th Avenue measured a wind gust of 50 knots / 58 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms pushing offshore produced tree damage in Brevard and St Lucie Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of limbs and small trees down in Palm Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms pushing offshore produced tree damage in Brevard and St Lucie Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage reported near Paradise Park on Juanita Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the influence of a mid-level trough to the north, South Florida was provided with a source of lift, which combined with surface instability from diurnal heating to develop strong to severe thunderstorms on the afternoon of July 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported up to quarter-size hail in Parkland along SR-7 as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud in Indian River County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter at the I-95 and Indrio Drive interchange reported a funnel cloud to the north of their location. Additional reports, including pictures and video, were received via social media. Location estimated to be northeast of the I-95 and Highway 60 interchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall in Florida's Big Bend region as a major Category 3 hurricane. It then moved northeastward, moving into southern Georgia, before pushing into the Atlantic off of the southeast US coast. The storm brought widespread tropical storm-force gusts to east central Florida and the adjacent waters, especially from Brevard and Osceola Counties northward. Rainfall was minimal, with the highest amount of 3.67 inches reported in Seminole County. Totals were around 3 inches from the Orlando metro northward. There were no reported tornadoes and the angle to the coast limited coastal erosion. There was one direct fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia's landfall along the Big Bend region led to widespread tropical storm force wind gusts through the day. The peak wind gust at the Space Coast Regional Airport was 33 knots. The Weatherflow site in the Banana River at 520 measured a 47 knot wind gust, as did Trident Pier, and the site at the Cocoa Beach Club measured a 43 knot wind gust. No damage was reported. One direct fatality occurred when a 60 year old man was windsurfing and drowned near Kelly Park in Merritt Island.

The storm brought widespread tropical storm-force gusts to east central Florida and the adjacent waters, especially from Brevard and Osceola Counties northward. Rainfall was minimal, with the highest amount of 3.67 inches reported in Seminole County. Totals were around 3 inches from the Orlando metro northward. There were no reported tornadoes and the angle to the coast limited coastal erosion. There was one direct fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia's landfall along the Big Bend region led to widespread tropical storm force wind gusts through the day. The peak wind gust was 40 knots at the Orlando Sanford International Airport. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front remains situated just to the northwest this morning with a light onshore flow near the surface while persistent southwesterly flow continues aloft. Cooler than normal mid-level temperatures created deeper instability that strengthened diurnally driven sea breeze storms as they develop along the east-coast sea breeze in the afternoon. Deeper moisture was across NE FL and portions of SE GA (generally south and east of Waycross) created more widespread storm coverage, especially along and west of the St Johns River basin as the sea breeze interacted with the river breeze. ||Strong to marginally severe wind gusts of 40-60 mph occurred. Marginal deep layer shear was around 20 kts which create a lower end hail threat. Locally heavy rainfall occurred that created mainly nuisance minor flood issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a landspout near Molasses Junction. Images were shared showing   roof damage to the sheet metal and wooden structure pf a horse stable and an outdoor toilet blown over. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved across north-Florida during the early morning hours as a warm front lifted up from central FL. Strong effective shear combined with marginal surface-based instability near the lifting warm front and surface low enabled rotating mini-supercells to form. Toward sunrise, enough low-level instability nosed inland from the Atlantic coast across Flagler County to support a couple of tornado touchdowns. Locally heavy rainfall occurred across northeast Florida, generally south of the I-10 corridor and toward the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The short-lived EF-2 tornado initially touched down near the Pine Lake Apartment complex, near the intersection of Belle Terre Pkwy and Pine Lakes Pkwy. The tornado traveled northeast through the B section of Palm Coast before dissipating near I-95. Several houses sustained significant roof  damage in the path, particularly the home along Baltimore Lane which suffered complete destruction of its roof. A minivan was also flipped onto its side and moved from the driveway onto the road along Birchwood Drive. Several screened lanai enclosures, sheds and fences were significantly damaged or destroyed. Several trees were snapped and powerlines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved through the area with ample instability and shear for severe weather. Numerous reports of severe weather were received across the tri-state area, although no tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at White Western Springs Road.
