EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak, sporadic tornado caused intermittent tree and minor structure damage as it track NE across the east-central part of the county. Tree damage was first reported near the intersection of County Roads 302 and 75 where several pines were snapped. Damage continued along a narrow ENE track which included a flagpole blown down in the R-section of Palm Coast at near the intersection of Rymshaw Drive and Ryman Lane. The last damage report was in Painters Hill where a resident reported a wind sail was shredded and patio furniture lofted and blown away.

Hurricane Idalia made landfall just to the north of the local area near Keaton Beach, FL in Taylor county in Florida's Big Bend as a category 3 hurricane and brought devastating storm surge and wind impacts all along the west Florida coast, especially in closer vicinity to the landfall area across the Nature Coast. Storm surge flooding reached into homes and businesses as far south as Lee county, and the surge inundation is the highest on record for the Cedar Key area.||County-by-County Impacts||Charlotte County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 55 to 65 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph near Charlotte Harbor at 1:49 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.11 inches near Harbour Heights. A peak water level of 3.57 feet above MHHW was measured at North Port Charlotte at 5:45 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Charlotte County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. US 41 was closed at Peace River Bridge due to flooding in downtown Punta Gorda, and 12 roadways were impassable due to saltwater inundation. 66 structures had major damage, and 14 had minor damage, with power outages affecting over 14,000.||Citrus County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 64 mph near Crystal River at 5:15 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 5 inches, with a maximum total of 5.2 inches near Inverness. A peak water level of 7.01 feet above MHHW was measured at the mouth of Crystal River near Shell Island at 8:45 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Citrus County generally ranged from 4 to 7 feet. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated numerous roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. Over 6,450 structures had inundation, with 72% being residential, 20% multi-family, and 8% commercial.||DeSoto County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 30 to 40 mph, with a maximum gust of 35 mph near Arcadia at 12:53 PM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 5 to 8 inches, with a maximum total of 7.11 inches near Arcadia. Power outages impacted 3,390.||Hardee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 4.64 inches near Ona.||Hernando County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 50 to 60 mph, with a maximum gust of 58 mph at Brooksville-Tampa Bay Regional Airport at 6:03 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.88 inches near Spring Hill. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. There were 115 structures with major damage, 39 with minor damage, and 1 structure was destroyed.||Highlands County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 40 to 50 mph, with a maximum gust of 47 mph at Sebring Regional Airport at 3:15 PM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.18 inches near Sebring. A tornado was observed near Avon Park, however no notable damage was reported.||Hillsborough County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 55 to 65 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph near Tampa International Airport at 3:39 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.26 inches near Riverview. The peak recorded water level of 6.56 feet (NAVD88) along the Alafia River at U.S. Highway 301 at 6:45 AM EST on August 30 surpassed the previous record of 6.2 feet, set as Hurricane Frances moved across the peninsula from the Atlantic on September 06, 2004. A peak water level of 4.18 feet above MHHW was measured at Old Port Tampa at 5:30 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Hillsborough County generally ranged from 3 to 5 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. 71 structures had major flood damage, and 10 had minor damage. The damage was mainly limited to surge/river flooding on the Alafia around US 301 and west to Tampa Bay, and the Little Manatee River between US 41 and Interstate 75.||Lee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 45 to 55 mph, with a maximum gust of 49 mph at Tarpon Point at 10:45 PM EST on August 29. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 5 inches, with a maximum total of 4.56 inches near Fort Myers. A peak water level of 3.2 feet above MHHW was measured at Fort Myers at 1:24 PM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Lee County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. There were no destroyed structures or ones with major damage. However, 51 had minor damage with 153 affected. Damage included scattered minor roof, siding, and soffit damage throughout the barrier islands from Captiva to Sanibel to Fort Myers Beach and through Bonita Beach. Minor salt water flooding in isolated homes near Bonita Beach, and Matlacha had 23 homes with minor flood damage.||Levy County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 69 mph at Cedar Key at 5:32 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.34 inches near Archer. The peak recorded water level of 6.89 feet above MHHW at Cedar Key at 7:48 AM EST on August 30 surpassed the previous record of 6.1 feet above MHHW, set during Hurricane Hermine on September 02, 2016. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Levy County generally ranged from 4 to 7 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated numerous roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront.||Manatee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 70 mph at Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport at 2:22 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.74 inches near Bradenton. A peak water level of 3.69 feet above MHHW was measured at Port Manatee at 7:54 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Manatee County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront and Riverfront through the downtown Bradenton area. 25 structures had major damage, and 2 had minor damage.||Pasco County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 45 to 55 mph, with a maximum gust of 45 mph near Hudson at 5:30 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches, with a maximum total of 4.05 inches near Wesley Chapel. One fatality occurred in Pasco county as the storm was approaching, due to a single vehicle automobile accident involving a 40-year-old male driving in the heavy rain and hitting a tree. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. There were 2,195 registrations for assistance, and 2,700cubic yards of debris were collected as of September 11th, with 480 tons of debris self-hauled.||Pinellas County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph at St. Petersburg-Clearwater International Airport at 3:43 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches, with a maximum total of 3.83 inches near Feather Sound. A peak water level of 4.05 feet above MHHW was measured at Clearwater Beach at 6:00 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Pinellas County generally ranged from 3 to 5 feet above MHHW. Over 70 water rescues were conducted, with nearly a dozen of those for pets, and 43 dogs and 29 cats were sheltered. Damage estimates currently total $23.4 million. 10 structures were destroyed, 45 inaccessible, 811 had major damage, 1,016 had minor damage, and 822 were affected. 27,000 were without power. The greatest impact was along the coastal areas of the county, including Shore Acres, Riviera Bay, Johns Pass to St. Pete Beach, and an area north of Dunedin.||Polk County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 40 to 50 mph, with a maximum gust of 47 mph at Winter Haven���s Gilbert Airport at 5:43 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 5.28 inches near Bartow.||Sarasota County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 62 mph near Sarasota at 12:18 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.5 inches near Sarasota. A peak water level of 3.08 feet above MHHW was measured at Venice at 1:30 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Sarasota County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. Damage is estimated at $2.7 million. There were 18 structures destroyed, with 1,100 with major damage. St. Armands Circle had inundation around 1 foot into businesses and homes surrounding it. The intersection of US 41 and Fruitville Road was closed due to storm surge flooding, Casey Key Road and Manasota Key Road were washed out, and a Tiki Bar on Sarasota Bayfront had its roof peeled back causing significant flooding. 8,000 homes were without power.||Sumter County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph, with a maximum gust of 36 mph near Bushnell at 5:54 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 2 inches, with a maximum total of 1.21 inches near Lady Lake. There were 8 road closures due to fallen trees and power lines, with 509 without power. On CR 740 at the RV Park, there was damage to the block wall and a pole barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 55 to 65 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph near Tampa International Airport at 3:39 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.26 inches near Riverview. A peak water level of 4.18 feet above MHHW was measured at Old Port Tampa at 5:30 AM EST on August 30. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. 71 structures had major flood damage, and 10 had minor damage. The damage was mainly limited to surge/river flooding on the Alafia around US 301 and west to Tampa Bay, and the Little Manatee River between US 41 and Interstate 75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate onshore flow led to numerous rip currents for a multi-day period. The strongest rip current occurred between June 15-24 when unfortunately several fatalities occurred.||On June 15th, according to a report from the Beach Police, a 52 year old male from Lula, GA drowned after he entered the Gulf to help two beachgoers who were fighting a rip current. The incident occurred at Public Beach Access 75, which is located near Front Beach Road and South Vestavia Street. Double red flags were flying.||On June 18th, according to a news release from the Panama City Beach Police Department, a 47 year old male from Helena, Alabama, drowned after he attempted to rescue his daughter from a rip current. The incident occurred near Tidewater Beach Condominiums, 16819 Front Beach Road. The daughter was rescued successfully. Single red flags were flying.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to a news release from the Panama City Beach Police Department, a 47 year old male from Helena, Alabama, drowned after he attempted to rescue his daughter from a rip current.  The incident occurred near Tidewater Beach Condominiums, 16819 Front Beach Road. The daughter was rescued successfully.  Single red flags were flying.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size fell in the Wacissa area near the Leon-Jefferson county line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near 16825 Highway 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of above average temperatures and above average dewpoints resulted in high heat index values in excess of 113 over a large portion of northwest Florida during the August 6-9 time period.

The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Highway 53 and County Road 253.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a funnel cloud near Cracker Swamp Road south of Hastings.

Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. There were 2,195 registrations for assistance, and 2,700cubic yards of debris were collected as of September 11th, with 480 tons of debris self-hauled.||Pinellas County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph at St. Petersburg-Clearwater International Airport at 3:43 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches, with a maximum total of 3.83 inches near Feather Sound. A peak water level of 4.05 feet above MHHW was measured at Clearwater Beach at 6:00 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Pinellas County generally ranged from 3 to 5 feet above MHHW. Over 70 water rescues were conducted, with nearly a dozen of those for pets, and 43 dogs and 29 cats were sheltered. Damage estimates currently total $23.4 million. 10 structures were destroyed, 45 inaccessible, 811 had major damage, 1,016 had minor damage, and 822 were affected. 27,000 were without power. The greatest impact was along the coastal areas of the county, including Shore Acres, Riviera Bay, Johns Pass to St. Pete Beach, and an area north of Dunedin.||Polk County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 40 to 50 mph, with a maximum gust of 47 mph at Winter Haven���s Gilbert Airport at 5:43 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 5.28 inches near Bartow.||Sarasota County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 62 mph near Sarasota at 12:18 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.5 inches near Sarasota. A peak water level of 3.08 feet above MHHW was measured at Venice at 1:30 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Sarasota County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. Damage is estimated at $2.7 million. There were 18 structures destroyed, with 1,100 with major damage. St. Armands Circle had inundation around 1 foot into businesses and homes surrounding it. The intersection of US 41 and Fruitville Road was closed due to storm surge flooding, Casey Key Road and Manasota Key Road were washed out, and a Tiki Bar on Sarasota Bayfront had its roof peeled back causing significant flooding. 8,000 homes were without power.||Sumter County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph, with a maximum gust of 36 mph near Bushnell at 5:54 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 2 inches, with a maximum total of 1.21 inches near Lady Lake. There were 8 road closures due to fallen trees and power lines, with 509 without power. On CR 740 at the RV Park, there was damage to the block wall and a pole barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 4.64 inches near Ona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph.

Hurricane Idalia made landfall just to the north of the local area near Keaton Beach, FL in Taylor county in Florida's Big Bend as a category 3 hurricane and brought devastating storm surge and wind impacts all along the west Florida coast, especially in closer vicinity to the landfall area across the Nature Coast. Storm surge flooding reached into homes and businesses as far south as Lee county, and the surge inundation is the highest on record for the Cedar Key area.||County-by-County Impacts||Charlotte County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 55 to 65 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph near Charlotte Harbor at 1:49 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.11 inches near Harbour Heights. A peak water level of 3.57 feet above MHHW was measured at North Port Charlotte at 5:45 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Charlotte County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. US 41 was closed at Peace River Bridge due to flooding in downtown Punta Gorda, and 12 roadways were impassable due to saltwater inundation. 66 structures had major damage, and 14 had minor damage, with power outages affecting over 14,000.||Citrus County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 64 mph near Crystal River at 5:15 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 5 inches, with a maximum total of 5.2 inches near Inverness. A peak water level of 7.01 feet above MHHW was measured at the mouth of Crystal River near Shell Island at 8:45 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Citrus County generally ranged from 4 to 7 feet. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated numerous roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. Over 6,450 structures had inundation, with 72% being residential, 20% multi-family, and 8% commercial.||DeSoto County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 30 to 40 mph, with a maximum gust of 35 mph near Arcadia at 12:53 PM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 5 to 8 inches, with a maximum total of 7.11 inches near Arcadia. Power outages impacted 3,390.||Hardee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 4.64 inches near Ona.||Hernando County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 50 to 60 mph, with a maximum gust of 58 mph at Brooksville-Tampa Bay Regional Airport at 6:03 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.88 inches near Spring Hill. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. There were 115 structures with major damage, 39 with minor damage, and 1 structure was destroyed.||Highlands County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 40 to 50 mph, with a maximum gust of 47 mph at Sebring Regional Airport at 3:15 PM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.18 inches near Sebring. A tornado was observed near Avon Park, however no notable damage was reported.||Hillsborough County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 55 to 65 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph near Tampa International Airport at 3:39 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.26 inches near Riverview. The peak recorded water level of 6.56 feet (NAVD88) along the Alafia River at U.S. Highway 301 at 6:45 AM EST on August 30 surpassed the previous record of 6.2 feet, set as Hurricane Frances moved across the peninsula from the Atlantic on September 06, 2004. A peak water level of 4.18 feet above MHHW was measured at Old Port Tampa at 5:30 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Hillsborough County generally ranged from 3 to 5 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. 71 structures had major flood damage, and 10 had minor damage. The damage was mainly limited to surge/river flooding on the Alafia around US 301 and west to Tampa Bay, and the Little Manatee River between US 41 and Interstate 75.||Lee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 45 to 55 mph, with a maximum gust of 49 mph at Tarpon Point at 10:45 PM EST on August 29. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 5 inches, with a maximum total of 4.56 inches near Fort Myers. A peak water level of 3.2 feet above MHHW was measured at Fort Myers at 1:24 PM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Lee County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. There were no destroyed structures or ones with major damage. However, 51 had minor damage with 153 affected. Damage included scattered minor roof, siding, and soffit damage throughout the barrier islands from Captiva to Sanibel to Fort Myers Beach and through Bonita Beach. Minor salt water flooding in isolated homes near Bonita Beach, and Matlacha had 23 homes with minor flood damage.||Levy County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 69 mph at Cedar Key at 5:32 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 3 inches, with a maximum total of 2.34 inches near Archer. The peak recorded water level of 6.89 feet above MHHW at Cedar Key at 7:48 AM EST on August 30 surpassed the previous record of 6.1 feet above MHHW, set during Hurricane Hermine on September 02, 2016. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Levy County generally ranged from 4 to 7 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated numerous roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront.||Manatee County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 70 mph at Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport at 2:22 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.74 inches near Bradenton. A peak water level of 3.69 feet above MHHW was measured at Port Manatee at 7:54 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Manatee County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront and Riverfront through the downtown Bradenton area. 25 structures had major damage, and 2 had minor damage.||Pasco County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 45 to 55 mph, with a maximum gust of 45 mph near Hudson at 5:30 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches, with a maximum total of 4.05 inches near Wesley Chapel. One fatality occurred in Pasco county as the storm was approaching, due to a single vehicle automobile accident involving a 40-year-old male driving in the heavy rain and hitting a tree. Elevated water levels submerged or partially inundated various roadways and structures along the immediate waterfront. There were 2,195 registrations for assistance, and 2,700cubic yards of debris were collected as of September 11th, with 480 tons of debris self-hauled.||Pinellas County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 61 mph at St. Petersburg-Clearwater International Airport at 3:43 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches, with a maximum total of 3.83 inches near Feather Sound. A peak water level of 4.05 feet above MHHW was measured at Clearwater Beach at 6:00 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels measured elsewhere along coastal Pinellas County generally ranged from 3 to 5 feet above MHHW. Over 70 water rescues were conducted, with nearly a dozen of those for pets, and 43 dogs and 29 cats were sheltered. Damage estimates currently total $23.4 million. 10 structures were destroyed, 45 inaccessible, 811 had major damage, 1,016 had minor damage, and 822 were affected. 27,000 were without power. The greatest impact was along the coastal areas of the county, including Shore Acres, Riviera Bay, Johns Pass to St. Pete Beach, and an area north of Dunedin.||Polk County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 40 to 50 mph, with a maximum gust of 47 mph at Winter Haven���s Gilbert Airport at 5:43 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with a maximum total of 5.28 inches near Bartow.||Sarasota County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 60 to 70 mph, with a maximum gust of 62 mph near Sarasota at 12:18 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 4 to 7 inches, with a maximum total of 6.5 inches near Sarasota. A peak water level of 3.08 feet above MHHW was measured at Venice at 1:30 AM EST on August 30. Peak water levels estimated elsewhere along coastal Sarasota County generally ranged from 2 to 4 feet above MHHW. Elevated water levels affected areas along the immediate waterfront. Damage is estimated at $2.7 million. There were 18 structures destroyed, with 1,100 with major damage. St. Armands Circle had inundation around 1 foot into businesses and homes surrounding it. The intersection of US 41 and Fruitville Road was closed due to storm surge flooding, Casey Key Road and Manasota Key Road were washed out, and a Tiki Bar on Sarasota Bayfront had its roof peeled back causing significant flooding. 8,000 homes were without power.||Sumter County - Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 35 to 45 mph, with a maximum gust of 36 mph near Bushnell at 5:54 AM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 1 to 2 inches, with a maximum total of 1.21 inches near Lady Lake. There were 8 road closures due to fallen trees and power lines, with 509 without power. On CR 740 at the RV Park, there was damage to the block wall and a pole barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations indicate peak wind gusts generally between 30 to 40 mph, with a maximum gust of 35 mph near Arcadia at 12:53 PM EST on August 30. Rainfall ranged from 5 to 8 inches, with a maximum total of 7.11 inches near Arcadia. Power outages impacted 3,390.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall on the morning of August 30th along the coast of Taylor County Florida near Keaton Beach at approximately 745 am EDT. The hurricane made landfall with estimated sustained winds of 125 mph, making it a Category 3 hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale, the first major hurricane on record to make landfall in this portion of Florida. Hurricane Idalia moved inland across the eastern Florida Big Bend and into South Central Georgia during the morning hours. Strong winds caused extensive damage to trees, buildings, and infrastructure along a broad swath stretching west to Tallahassee and east beyond the Suwannee River. Significant and devastating storm surge accompanied the hurricane into the Taylor and Dixie County coasts, where water levels rose considerably, inundating numerous structures along the coast and flooding residences and businesses many miles inland. Significant damage to property and infrastructure was associated with the storm surge that will take a considerable time to rebuild. The peak surge values observed were from Dekle Beach in Taylor County southeastward to Horseshoe Beach in Dixie County. Surge heights in this area along the immediate coast were within the range of 7 to 12 feet above normally dry ground. Lower values of up to 6 feet above normally dry ground were noted south of Horseshoe Beach near the community of Suwannee. It should also be noted the storm made landfall around the time of low tide. Had the storm made landfall 6 hours later, around the time of high tide, peak water level values could have been between 3-4 feet higher.  In the process of the high water mark surveys, interviews were conducted with several residents that lived in the area during the Storm of the Century, a non-tropical system that affected this portion of the Florida coastline on March 13, 1993. All of these residents interviewed from Keaton Beach to Horseshoe Beach indicated water levels that rivaled or exceeded those experienced in the 1993 ���Storm of the Century.��� Moreover, Dixie County Emergency Management noted that the inland extent of the storm surge from Hurricane Idalia moved much further inland from Horseshoe Beach than observed in The Storm of the Century. One direct fatality occurred in south-central Georgia as a result of the storm.||Damage estimates from Florida include $216.1 million spread over 21,525 insurance claims as of late September. An additional $447.9 million in agricultural losses was estimated by the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services. Hurricane Idalia made landfall on the morning of August 30th along the coast of Taylor County Florida near Keaton Beach at approximately 745 am EDT. The hurricane made landfall with estimated sustained winds of 125 mph, making it a Category 3 hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale, the first major hurricane on record to make landfall in this portion of Florida. Hurricane Idalia moved inland across the eastern Florida Big Bend and into South Central Georgia during the morning hours. Strong winds caused extensive damage to trees, buildings, and infrastructure along a broad swath stretching west to Tallahassee and east beyond the Suwannee River. Significant and devastating storm surge accompanied the hurricane into the Taylor and Dixie County coasts, where water levels rose considerably, inundating numerous structures along the coast and flooding residences and businesses many miles inland. Significant damage to property and infrastructure was associated with the storm surge that will take a considerable time to rebuild. The peak surge values observed were from Dekle Beach in Taylor County southeastward to Horseshoe Beach in Dixie County. Surge heights in this area along the immediate coast were within the range of 7 to 12 feet above normally dry ground. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall on the morning of August 30th along the coast of Taylor County Florida near Keaton Beach at approximately 745 am EDT. The hurricane made landfall with estimated sustained winds of 125 mph, making it a Category 3 hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale, the first major hurricane on record to make landfall in this portion of Florida. Hurricane Idalia moved inland across the eastern Florida Big Bend and into South Central Georgia during the morning hours. Strong winds caused extensive damage to trees, buildings, and infrastructure along a broad swath stretching west to Tallahassee and east beyond the Suwannee River. Significant and devastating storm surge accompanied the hurricane into the Taylor and Dixie County coasts, where water levels rose considerably, inundating numerous structures along the coast and flooding residences and businesses many miles inland. Significant damage to property and infrastructure was associated with the storm surge that will take a considerable time to rebuild. The peak surge values observed were from Dekle Beach in Taylor County southeastward to Horseshoe Beach in Dixie County. Surge heights in this area along the immediate coast were within the range of 7 to 12 feet above normally dry ground. Lower values of up to 6 feet above normally dry ground were noted south of Horseshoe Beach near the community of Suwannee. It should also be noted the storm made landfall around the time of low tide. Had the storm made landfall 6 hours later, around the time of high tide, peak water level values could have been between 3-4 feet higher.  In the process of the high water mark surveys, interviews were conducted with several residents that lived in the area during the Storm of the Century, a non-tropical system that affected this portion of the Florida coastline on March 13, 1993. All of these residents interviewed from Keaton Beach to Horseshoe Beach indicated water levels that rivaled or exceeded those experienced in the 1993 ���Storm of the Century.��� Moreover, Dixie County Emergency Management noted that the inland extent of the storm surge from Hurricane Idalia moved much further inland from Horseshoe Beach than observed in The Storm of the Century. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW steering flow pushed the dominant west coast sea breeze inland with a merger between the Highway 301 and St. Johns River basin during the evening. A moist and unstable airmass near the collision produced a few strong to pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a power line off of County Road 13 South. The time of damage was based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a power line off of County Road 210. The time of damage was based on radar.  The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW steering flow pushed the dominant west coast sea breeze inland with a merger between the Highway 301 and St. Johns River basin during the evening. A moist and unstable airmass near the collision produced a few strong to pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rip current rescue occurred at 3385 North Oceanshore. A moderate rip current was advertised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW steering flow pushed the dominant west coast sea breeze inland with a merger between the Highway 301 and St. Johns River basin during the evening. A moist and unstable airmass near the collision produced a few strong to pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St Johns County Marine Rescue reported a rip current drowning about 1.5 miles north of the Fort Matanzas Beach access area. There were 3 others there were rescued and transported to a hospital. There was a large rip current in the area. Surf was about 1-2 feet and semi-choppy. NWS Jacksonville had a moderate rip current risk advertised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sea breeze storms over a very moist and unstable airmass produced localized strong to severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station at the Jacksonville Terminal Channel measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wavering surface boundary across SE Georgia, the dominant Gulf Coast sea breeze and steep mid and upper-level lapse rates combined with some dry pockets of low-level air produced scattered diurnally enhanced strong to severe storms that produced wind gusts of 40-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A teenager under a tree was injured by a lightning strike in Switzerland. The time of injury was based on radar and lightning data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wavering surface boundary across SE Georgia, the dominant Gulf Coast sea breeze and steep mid and upper-level lapse rates combined with some dry pockets of low-level air produced scattered diurnally enhanced strong to severe storms that produced wind gusts of 40-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Pine Circle West. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow pushed sea breeze storms offshore of the local Atlantic coast in the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted just north of the Dames Point Bridge, heading east along Zoo Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow and a warm, unstable airmass triggered strong to isolated severe pulse storms during the afternoon and evening when sea breezes and boundaries collided. Storms drifted toward the Atlantic coast into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down across Terrell Road near the Jacksonville International Airport. The time damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow and a warm, unstable airmass triggered strong to isolated severe pulse storms during the afternoon and evening when sea breezes and boundaries collided. Storms drifted toward the Atlantic coast into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a power line. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow and a warm, unstable airmass triggered strong to isolated severe pulse storms during the afternoon and evening when sea breezes and boundaries collided. Storms drifted toward the Atlantic coast into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line and power pole were blown down on top of a tractor. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong storm preceded a cold front, then trailing the frontal passage overnight, stronger non-thunderstorm wind gusts of 40-55 mph caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The broadcast media reported a semi-truck jack-knifed due to strong wind gusts along I-295 northbound into a concrete barrier. All lanes of I-295 northbound north of the Trout River were blocked for a couple of hours. Gusty winds of 45-55 mph were reported across the county in a line of showers along a cold front around the time of damage. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper-level trough moved over the Florida Peninsula, abundant low-level moisture and diurnal heating led to plenty of instability in the atmosphere on May 24th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast meteorologist and county emergency managers reported several trees down and fence damage in Clewiston in the area near Clewiston Middle School and extending south to Sykes Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the influence of a mid-level trough to the north, South Florida was provided with a source of lift, which combined with surface instability from diurnal heating to develop scattered showers and thunderstorms on the afternoon of May 25th. One of these thunderstorms produced a brief tornado in southwest Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a short-lived EF-0 tornado in Redland, starting around|SW 192nd St before traveling south along SW 147th Ave until almost SW 200th St. Several trees were snapped and/or uprooted. A semi tractor trailer was knocked on its side by the tornado near SW 196th St and the driver was injured. Most of the winds along the brief damage path were estimated in the 55-65 mph range, with a peak estimate of 80 mph. Damage amounts are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a mid-level trough persisted over the Florida Panhandle, abundant low-level moisture and diurnal heating led to plenty of instability in the atmosphere on May 26th. Numerous slow-moving showers and thunderstorms formed in the afternoon across the Miami metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media member reported flooding across a few roadways and parking lots in South Miami Heights due to heavy rainfall in the area. Rainfall amounts were in the 2 to 4 inch range, most of it falling in 2 hours or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal flood advisory due to strong onshore and high astronomical tides was in effect for the local Atlantic coast which caused minor tidal flooding inundation near high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding occurred in Flagler Beach near high tide. South Flagler Avenue at South 23rd Street was under water but still passable via reports from trained storm spotters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front stalled near the FL-GA state line as a broad area of low pressure moved along the front and across north Florida through the day. Above normal precipitable water content (> 1.8 inches) combined with low level isentropic lift and a strong SW low level jet near 35 kts produced locally heavy rainfall of 2-3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Eddy Tower RAWS measured 2.92 inches in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized heavy rainfall occurred across some coastal areas as a broad surface low moved onshore north of Nassau County Florida. South to southwest winds around the low across NE FL focused sea breeze convection toward the Atlantic coast in the afternoon over a moist and unstable airmass where locally heavy rain caused minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported along Bird of Paradise Drive and along side streets in Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist, unsettled SW steering flow and low-level convergence brought an early start to storms across the Suwannee River Valley. A short-wave trough impulse approached from the west in the afternoon over an unstable airmass, that further enhanced convective growth as storms approached the Atlantic coast. A strengthening low-level jet prevented the east coast sea breeze from moving far inland but did enhance downdraft strength in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the Haddock Drive and Haddock Road intersection. The time of damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level ridge extended over the region. Light steering flow enabled sea breezes to develop and move inland into the afternoon with a few to isolated strong pulse severe storms along boundary mergers over a very moist and unstable airmass. Local heat advisories were in effect for heat index values 108-112 degrees. A high rip current risk continued at local beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flagler Beach Ocean Rescue assisted with a rip current rescue at North 10th Street in Flagler Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road was almost washed out and impassable due to flood water at Turner Cemetery Road and JD Smith Trail. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW steering flow pushed the dominant west coast sea breeze inland with a merger between the Highway 301 and St. Johns River basin during the evening. A moist and unstable airmass near the collision produced a few strong to pulse severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a large fire at the Summer Grove condo complex near the intersection of San Jose Blvd and Old St Augustine Road. Eight units were damaged and 20 people affected. There were no injuries reported. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data. With 8 units being damage, an estimated $5 of damage to each unit was used in Storm Data (this is likely a gross underestimate).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding was reported along DeBarry Avenue. This is a known flood prone location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave troughs crossing the region, and cooler temperatures aloft, led to the ideal environment for an active afternoon and evening on May 19th with widespread showers and thunderstorms. Ample diurnal heating increased instability across the region which lead to numerous strong thunderstorms with the primary hazards of gusty winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel sized hail was reported by the public in Wellington near the Polo Country Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong ENE winds between strong high pressure north of the region and deepening low pressure in the Gulf of Mexico approaching the Florida peninsula created strong onshore wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a home and car in Mandarin. At the time of damage, wind gusts of 40-45 mph were reported due to strengthening onshore winds. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home attic in Orange Park during the afternoon which resulted in a fire. Extensive damage occurred to the home and the structure was deemed uninhabitable. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud lasting over 5 minutes naer the Opa-Locka Airport, associated with a passing shower.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms formed in the afternoon and evening across South Florida with the primary hazards of damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ham radio operator reported a large tree and power lines down on Lantana Road near Jog Road as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved through the area with ample instability and shear for severe weather. Numerous reports of severe weather were received across the tri-state area.  Two tornadoes occurred across southwest Georgia, and seven tornadoes occurred across the eastern Florida panhandle and big bend.  An EF2 tornado produced significant damage in the town of Hosford, FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that he saw a tornado briefly touch down along Highway 71 with downed trees.  This tornado was rated EF0 due to the tree damage. No structures were impacted in this rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper-level trough moved over the Florida Peninsula, abundant low-level moisture and diurnal heating led to plenty of instability in the atmosphere on May 24th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms formed in the afternoon and evening across South Florida with the primary hazards of damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glades Central High School Earth Networks station measured a wind gust to 62 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged a dock at Big Coppitt Key as a tropical wave moved west through the Florida Keys, accompanied by scattered thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to a dock damaged a concrete piling.  Photos relayed through social media showed cracks and missing pieces of the plastic casing, as well as to an interior concrete post in two separate areas above and below the wooden dock level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm flipped over planes at the Kissimmee Gateway Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported passed on from Florida Department of Emergency Management of two planes flipped over at Kissimmee Gateway Airport due to high wind gusts, causing a fuel spill. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man drowned in a rip current in Wilbur-by-the-Sea a female companion was able to escape uninjured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County Beach Safety reported a 37 year-old male and a female companion from out of state were caught in a rip current around 4pm. The female was able to return to shore and call for help. Beach safety personnel recovered the victim from beyond the sandbar. He was then transported to a local hospital and pronounced deceased. The incident occurred at an unguarded section of the beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved through the area with ample instability and shear for severe weather. Numerous reports of severe weather were received across the tri-state area.  Two tornadoes occurred across southwest Georgia, and seven tornadoes occurred across the eastern Florida panhandle and big bend.  An EF2 tornado produced significant damage in the town of Hosford, FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A corridor of intense straight line winds estimated near 80 mph moved from far Eastern Gadsden County into Northern Leon County. Damage was first noted with several trees down along Concord Road in Gadsden County. After crossing the Ochlockonee River, the area of intense winds uprooted or snapped several trees along Meridian Road about 2 miles north of Orchard Pond Parkway. The wind damage continued through Luna Plantation subdivision west of Bull Headley Road and along the south shore of Lake Iamonia. Damage in this area was large pines snapping or uprooting. One home was impacted in Luna Plantation subdivision by a falling tree. Tree limbs and other debris from the west side of Bull Headley Road impacted homes near the Chadwick Way entrance to Killearn Lakes. Four homes along the west side of Membrook Lane had roof damage, with one residence having damage to a car as well. Sporadic wind damage continued along the south shore of Lake Iamonia with several large pine trees snapped along Journeys End Road. A couple of homes sustained roof damage from tree impacts. Maximum winds of 80 mph are believed to have occurred in this portion of the damage path. A final area of damage occurred along US-319 near Cherokee Plantation Road where numerous trees were uprooted or snapped. One tree fell on a car causing 1 injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the area with a few storms producing wind damage across Jefferson and Wakulla counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Wakulla county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wake low following the passage of thunderstorms associated with a mesoscale convective system ahead of a surface low pressure system over the central Gulf of Mexico produced a non-thunderstorm high wind gust near Fort Myers in Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS site KRSW Southwest Florida International Airport reported a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low-pressure system moving across the Florida Keys brought widespread heavy rainfall to much of South Florida from the afternoon of November 15th to the early morning hours of November 16th. Strong winds were also observed across South Florida during this time frame as a wake low developed on the backside of the system's precipitation field. These strong winds also led to coastal flooding along portions of the SE Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of standing water on roads in the Miami Lakes and Hialeah areas due to heavy rainfall as continuous rounds of showers moved through the area. There were no reports of structures being impacted. 10 to 12 inches of rainfall was estimated to have fallen over a 12-hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds across the area ahead of a cold front over North Florida caused temperatures to be above normal across SE Florida, with high temperatures in the lower 90s and heat index values around 100 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of people were treated for heat-related illness at the J'ouvert festival in Lauderhill. A total of 54 people required treatment, with 26 requiring transport to local hospitals. It is believed that alcohol consumption and medical-related issues were factors along with the above normal temperatures.||The high temperature at the nearest ASOS at Ft. Lauderdale Executive Airport was 92F, which is 5 degrees above normal for the date, with a maximum heat index of 100F registered that afternoon. A nearby WeatherSTEM station recorded heat index of 98F or higher for 5 hours, with a max value of 103F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high monthly astronomical tides, also known as king tides, in combination with the strong onshore winds and increasing northeast swell caused by strong high pressure over the eastern United States, brought minor coastal flooding to  portions of Broward and Miami-Dade Counties. Winds reached gusts of 25 to 30 knots during the morning high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local emergency manager indicated minor coastal flooding with the morning high tide in Miami Beach. Water was reaching levels above the seawall and approaching the streets near the intersection of Collins Avenue and 23rd Street. Tide levels at nearby Virginia Key were 1.65 feet MHHW, or 2.82 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system near the Florida peninsula brought strong gradient winds and flooding rainfall to east central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management confirmed a total of ten buildings with water intrusion in Fellsmere and Vero Lake Estates. Also reports that all lanes of 101st Ave were closed at 85th St in Vero Lake Estates. There was a petroleum spill due to eight stalled vehicles. In addition, all lanes of 79th St were closed in Fellsmere and two vehicles were stalled in Fellsmere due to the flooding rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather episode with an area of low pressure and associated cold front moving east across the Gulf of Mexico approaching the peninsula produced multiple supercell thunderstorms and likely waterspouts over the open waters before approaching the west Florida coast, where one waterspout was observed and multiple strong wind gusts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very brief tornado was observed over the intracoastal waterway along the Belleair Beach causeway at 11:34 AM EST (1534Z). At 11:35 AM EST (1535Z), the tornado moved onshore and damaged the roof of an apartment building before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable air along a stalled frontal boundary over South Florida led to the formation of slow-moving showers and thunderstorms during the evening hours of November 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water and stranded vehicles were reported along several streets in Pompano Beach and Oakland Park due to heavy rainfall after continuous rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved across the area. Up to 6 inches of standing water was reported along portions of State Road A1A with multiple stranded vehicles. 4 to 6 inches of rain was estimated to have fallen within a 3-hour period across this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate onshore flow led to numerous rip currents for a multi-day period. The strongest rip current occurred between June 15-24 where unfortunately several fatalities occurred.||On June 21st, a 53 year old male from South Carolina drowned in a rip current behind Pelican Walk Rentals, 6905 Thomas Drive. Double red flags were flying.||On June 22nd, a 65 year old male from Coppell, TX drowned from a rip current. Beach patrons notified South Walton Fire District's Tower 31 lifeguard that bystanders had pulled the male, who was unresponsive, from the water nearly half a mile to the west of the tower. Single red flags were flying.  In addition, a 47 year old male from Decatur, TN died trying to save his girlfriend from a rip current.  The incident occurred about 2:20 p.m. CT near the Flamingo Hotel and Tower at 15525 Front Beach Road. Double red flags were posted at that time.||On June 24th, a 58 year old male from Nashville, TN drowned in a rip current just offshore of Scenic Gulf Drive in Walton county. Single red flags were flying.  In addition, the Panama City Beach Police Department reported three separate fatal water incidents behind three different resorts on Front Beach Road. Officers arrived on scene at the first incident at 12:04 p.m. to find PCB Beach Safety lifeguards actively rescuing a female from the Gulf of Mexico behind Days Inn located at 12818 Front Beach Road. She was successfully brought to shore but was unresponsive and life-saving measures were started. She was transported to a local hospital where she was pronounced deceased. The victim was a 39 year old female from Lithonia, Georgia. Officers arrived on scene at a second incident to find PCB Beach Safety lifeguards actively rescuing a male from the Gulf of Mexico behind Boardwalk Beach Resort located at 9450 South Thomas Drive at 1:13 p.m. He was successfully brought to shore but was unresponsive and life-saving measures were started. He was transported to a local hospital where he was pronounced deceased. The victim was identified as a 63 year old male from Lithia Springs, Georgia. Officers arrived on scene at a third incident to find PCB Beach Safety lifeguards rescuing a male from the Gulf of Mexico behind Emerald Isle Condos located at 17545 Front Beach Road at 4:23 p.m. He was successfully brought to shore unresponsive. Life-saving measures were started. He was transported to a local hospital where he was pronounced deceased. The victim was identified as a 68 year old male, from Canton, Michigan. Single red flags were flying at the times of the first two incidents in Bay county with double red flags at the time of the third incident in Bay county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of stratiform rain moved across the region during the early morning hours of October 12th. Gravity waves were apparent on TLH radar, and wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were briefly measured across extreme southern Georgia and the Florida big bend that were not associated with thunderstorms. Isolated wind damage occurred to a storefront in Perry with winds perhaps briefly gusting near 60 mph based on the damage photos. No other damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media submitted pictures of damage to a storefront in Perry. A large area of stratiform rain with no lightning was moving through the area with gravity waves apparent on TLH radar. Winds perhaps briefly gusted near 60 mph based on the damage photos. No other damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of high moisture values in a south-southwest wind flow pattern led to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms over SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a mobile home in Davie during a heavy thunderstorm, igniting a fire, which resulted in a complete loss of the structure. Three people were displaced due to the damage. Time is based on National Lightning Detection Network data which showed several lightning strikes in the immediate area. Damage totals are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active period of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms resulted in several reports of wind damage.  Most of the damage occurred on September 7th as a cluster of severe storms moved through Tallahassee.  In addition, a significant downburst with winds estimated around 85 mph occurred near Spring Creek in Wakulla county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a power line near Sunflower Road and Celia Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest mid-level flow on the backside of a trough exiting the area ushered cool and dry air across South Florida, but there was enough low-level moisture to support a few showers across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a funnel cloud near Belle Glade as a shower passed over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper-level trough over the Florida Peninsula created an early start for convection as showers and storms began to develop in the morning hours over the Atlantic Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported a brief funnel cloud over Highway A1A in Jupiter as a shower passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dissipating front across Central Florida along with a wide area of moisture streaming northward across the region led to the formation of locally heavy showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours across SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Fort Lauderdale, mainly in the Edgewood and Melrose Manors neighborhoods. Up to 2.5 inches of rain was measured in this area, and this rain fell in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary situated over Central Florida brought a warm and moist airmass over South Florida, which combined with ample lift from mid-level troughing to develop strong thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening. These storms produced strong wind gusts over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherSTEM/FSWN station at Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport measured a wind gust to 57 knots / 66 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through the area. Wind gusts up to 55 mph were measured at the same location at 6:53 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary situated over northern Florida brought a warm and very moist airmass over South Florida, which combined with ample lift from mid-level troughing to develop widespread slow-moving showers and thunderstorms over the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported street flooding due to heavy rainfall in a portion of metro Palm Beach County that included Wellington, Florida Gardens, and Greenacres. Broadcast media shared a video of significant street flooding in Tropical Country Estates near Lyons Road, with water getting into the front yards of homes. A total of 6-8 inches of rain fell in this area, with most of the rain falling in about 2 hours. No structures were reported to be impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The late September astronomical high tides (King Tide) in combination with onshore winds and NE swell brought moderate coastal flooding along the Atlantic Coast of South Florida. This flooding continued into the first few days of October, and another episode will be included in the October report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported saltwater flooding in Miami Beach along the Intracoastal Waterway and Biscayne Bay shorelines during the high tide cycles. Standing water was less than 1 foot in parking lots and on the shoulders of roads. Tide levels at nearby Virginia Key peaked at 1.7 feet MHHW or 3.94 feet MLLW at 10:30 AM EDT September 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The late September astronomical high tides (King Tide) in combination with onshore winds and NE swell brought moderate coastal flooding along the Atlantic Coast of South Florida. This flooding continued into the first few days of October, and another episode will be included in the October report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported via social media of saltwater flooding on Flagler Drive on the western side of the Intracoastal Waterway in West Palm Beach. Standing water was less than 1 foot on nearby sidewalks and parking lots. Tide levels at nearby Lake Worth Pier peaked at 1.94 feet MHHW or 5.0 feet MLLW at 9:06 AM on September 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The late September astronomical high tides (King Tide) in combination with onshore winds and NE swell brought moderate coastal flooding along the Atlantic Coast of South Florida. This flooding continued into the first few days of October, and another episode will be included in the October report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public shared pictures and videos of street flooding in Key Biscayne resulting from a combination of rainfall and high tides. Standing water of less than 1 foot was observed on roads, driveways, and backyards. Tide levels at nearby Virginia Key peaked at 1.59 feet MHHW or 3.83 feet MLLW. Rainfall in the area was around 0.9 inches during the time of the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public shared pictures of saltwater flooding in NE Miami near Northeast 10th Avenue and Northeast 79th Street. Standing water of 1 foot or less was observed on roads and sidewalks. Tide levels at nearby Virginia Key peaked at 1.7 feet MHHW or 3.94 feet MLLW at 10:30 AM EDT September 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall in a short period of time, leading to areas of flash flooding in Washington county and on St George Island.  Over 4 inches of rain was measured in Washington county and almost 7 inches of rain was measured on St George Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Pioneer Road and Gainer Road was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developing in association with a deep-layered cyclonic circulation near the Yucatan Peninsula produced isolated severe thunderstorm wind gusts at the Key West International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A southwest wind gust of 59 mph was measured at Key West International Airport.  Power outages affecting just over 1,500 customers were reported, mostly in the Marathon, FL vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall in a short period of time, leading to areas of flash flooding in Washington county and on St George Island. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Mahan at Phillips.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down south of Perry, FL on the north side of Houck Rd. Here the tornado produced EF-0 damage to a large metal building system, pulling some of the metal roof panels off the building and sending insulation over to Perry Foley Airport. Traveling southeast across Houck Rd, EF-1 damage was observed as the tornado then flipped up the metal roof of a porch, peeled back the metal roof of an outbuilding, snapped a tree and uprooted a very large tree. Further southeast at the airport a sign was damaged, debris from the metal building was found, and one aircraft broke free of its tail rope and was pushed sideways. The tornado then lifted shortly past the airport. Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several videos posted on social media indicated that a tornado touched down on the Choctawhatchee Bay just west of the Sandestin Resort. Initial damage included damage to a storm shutter on the condo directly facing the Bay, and isolated snapped and toppled trees between the Bay and Baytowne Avenue West. This damage was consistent with EF0 damage. EF1 damage was observed east of Baytowne Avenue, where several hardwood trees were uprooted in multiple directions on Turnberry Court. After crossing a golf course and small unpopulated area, the highest concentration of damage occurred along Laurel Way and Ravens Run, where multiple trees were snapped and uprooted with minor roof damage noted to a few homes. The tornado continued east causing minor roof damage to a home on Olde Towne Avenue in addition to uprooting a few hardwood trees. The tornado continued to the east of the resort, snapping and uprooting trees on Mack Bayou Road and east Mack Bayou Drive where several fences sustained damage in addition to minor damage to the roof of a small business. The tornado then lifted east of Mack Bayou Drive. Damage was consistent with an EF1 max wind speed of 105 mph.

Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on NW County Road 275.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front remains situated just to the northwest this morning with a light onshore flow near the surface while persistent southwesterly flow continues aloft. Cooler than normal mid-level temperatures created deeper instability that strengthened diurnally driven sea breeze storms as they develop along the east-coast sea breeze in the afternoon. Deeper moisture was across NE FL and portions of SE GA (generally south and east of Waycross) created more widespread storm coverage, especially along and west of the St Johns River basin as the sea breeze interacted with the river breeze. ||Strong to marginally severe wind gusts of 40-60 mph occurred. Marginal deep layer shear was around 20 kts which create a lower end hail threat. Locally heavy rainfall occurred that created mainly nuisance minor flood issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down at 1506 Husson Avenue. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines on Highway 71 near the Five Acre Farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface front was near the FL-GA state-line with moist, unstable conditions south of the boundary across NE FL. Diurnally enhanced storms further intensified into the afternoon and early evening as a short-wave trough moved across the area from the west. Storm motion was slow, so locally heavy rainfall was a concern near storm mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station just west of Atlantic Beach Elementary School measured storm total rainfall of 4.65 inches by 8 pm. Other public weather stations in the area measured 3-4.5 inches of daily rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved across north-Florida during the early morning hours as a warm front lifted up from central FL. Strong effective shear combined with marginal surface-based instability near the lifting warm front and surface low enabled rotating mini-supercells to form. Toward sunrise, enough low-level instability nosed inland from the Atlantic coast across Flagler County to support a couple of tornado touchdowns. Locally heavy rainfall occurred across northeast Florida, generally south of the I-10 corridor and toward the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along County Road 75. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled WSW steering flow and elevated moisture of 1.7 inches across SE GA and portions of N Florida triggered scattered strong to isolated severe storms during the afternoon and evening along sea breeze and boundary collisions over an unstable low level airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to dime size hail was reported in Quail Hollow section of Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall Wednesday morning August 30th around 745 AM EDT along the|Florida Big Bend near Keaton Beach as a category 3, with the inner core moving NE across the|Suwannee River Valley. The inner wind core of Idalia gradually weakened through the morning while,|heavily impacting Suwannee and Hamilton counties in NE Florida and Echols county SE Georgia as the|circulation tracked NE. Widespread tree, power line and structure damage occurred across Suwannee and Hamilton counties, with estimated winds of 80-100 mph based on damage surveys with isolated pockets of peak wind gusts near major hurricane strength. Outer rainbands on the eastside of Idalia|impacted all of SE Georgia and NE FL through the day, with early morning tornadoes across coastal SE Georgia and widespread tropical cyclone wind gusts through the day causing tree damage and powerline damage at least somewhere across NE Florida and SE Georgia. Measured gusts during the peak of the hurricane across the local forecast area were 60-80 mph. Flooding rainfall impacted locations in the direct path of the weakening inner core across the Suwannee River Valley and southeast Georgia,|especially from eastern Hamilton county toward Baxley and Jesup GA. The peak rainfall amounts were up to 5-8 inches. At least 3 tornadoes occurred in southeast GA as rainbands swept northwestward ahead of the hurricane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical cyclone winds impacted the county on 8/30. Trees and power lines were blown down.|At 0600, a 59-year-old man died when he crashed into a tree near State Road 20 and Southeast 60th Terrace,|in Gainesville due to heavy rainfall. At 0710, a spotter reported a large tree and power lines were blown|down about 3 miles NNE of Newberry. At 0720, the ASOS KGNV at the Gainesville Regional Airport measured|a 40 mph wind gust. At 0750, a mesonet station about 3 miles SE of the University of Florida measured a|wind gust of 45 mph at the Sweetwater Wetlands Park. At 0830, a mesonet site about 1 mile NE of the|University of Florida measured a wind gust of 51 mph. More than 30 downed trees and power outages were|reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal hazard potential increased through the week as high astronomical spring tides coincided with a persistent onshore flow pattern. Coastal hazards include increasingly strong and frequent rip currents, beach erosion and minor to moderate tidal flooding along the coast, within the Intracoastal Waterway and St. Johns River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS tide site MYPF1 at Mayport recorded a peak tide value of 1.93 ft MHHW datum on 9/27 at 9:18 pm. Minor tidal flooding begins at 1.8 ft MHHW. At 8:54 pm on 9/28, the gage measured a peak water level of 1.99 ft MHHW. Moderate tidal flooding begins at 2.3 ft MHHW. At 10:00 am on 9/30, the tide gage reached 2.09 ft MHHW. At 10:15 am on 9/30, a NWS employee reported tidal flood water surrounded buildings and covered parking lots near Hecksher Drive and Browns Creek. At 10:54 am, the tide gage in the Dames Point Bridge on the St. Johns River reached 2.04 ft MHHW. Minor tidal flooding begins at this location at 1.6 ft MHHW. At 11:42 am on 9/30, the tide gage at the Main Street Bridge reached 1.61 ft MHHW. Minor tidal flooding begins at 1.5 ft MHHW at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering front across NE FL combined with light NNE steering flow and sea breeze convergence near the frontal zone in the afternoon and evening created locally heavy rainfall across Marion county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore NNE winds and elevated high astronomical tides created an elevated rip current risk at local beaches. A moderate rip current risk of rip currents was advertised.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five people were rescued at North 11th Street and A1A along in Flagler Beach. A separate rescue of 1 person occurred near the Flagler Beach pier. There were no sustained injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall Wednesday morning August 30th around 745 AM EDT along the|Florida Big Bend near Keaton Beach as a category 3, with the inner core moving NE across the|Suwannee River Valley. The inner wind core of Idalia gradually weakened through the morning while,|heavily impacting Suwannee and Hamilton counties in NE Florida and Echols county SE Georgia as the|circulation tracked NE. Widespread tree, power line and structure damage occurred across Suwannee and Hamilton counties, with estimated winds of 80-100 mph based on damage surveys with isolated pockets of peak wind gusts near major hurricane strength. Outer rainbands on the eastside of Idalia|impacted all of SE Georgia and NE FL through the day, with early morning tornadoes across coastal SE Georgia and widespread tropical cyclone wind gusts through the day causing tree damage and powerline damage at least somewhere across NE Florida and SE Georgia. Measured gusts during the peak of the hurricane across the local forecast area were 60-80 mph. Flooding rainfall impacted locations in the direct path of the weakening inner core across the Suwannee River Valley and southeast Georgia,|especially from eastern Hamilton county toward Baxley and Jesup GA. The peak rainfall amounts were up to 5-8 inches. At least 3 tornadoes occurred in southeast GA as rainbands swept northwestward ahead of the hurricane.

A rare Extreme Wind Warning product was issued by the NWS|Jacksonville as the eyewall of Major Hurricane strength moved across the county during the morning.|There were barns, carports and other out-buildings destroyed with major roof loss and damage along 221st|Road and along 148th Street.
